```yaml
  description: "No one is buying your product because of how they will think. They buy because of how they will feel. They rationalize the purchase by thinking. We are all emotional creatures dealing in the lizard/mammalian brain area."
  domains: ["sales-psychology", "buyer-behavior", "copywriting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-595"
  title: "Inquiry Cures Judgment"
  description: "Questions in copy build rapport without judgment. When you ask 'Did you know?' and 'Have you ever thought about?', you're building rapport. The reader doesn't feel judged. Sales pages with no judgment in the first paragraphs convert better."
  domains: ["copywriting", "rapport-building", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-596"
  title: "Clicks Don't Matter â€” Buys Matter"
  description: "People are so concerned about clicks. I'm more concerned about buys. Getting a click is not the answer â€” the buy is the answer."
  domains: ["conversion-optimization", "marketing-philosophy", "metrics"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-597"
  title: "Your Life's Work In A Handbook"
  description: "When you can truthfully say 'my life's work is found within this', it's very powerful. People feel bad saying it's too expensive when you frame it as your life's dedication. It justifies price through personal sacrifice narrative."
  domains: ["copywriting", "pricing-psychology", "value-framing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-598"
  title: "Standing On Shoulders Of Giants Creates Trust"
  description: "Saying 'I can't take all the credit' appeals to everyone's desire to like people who are modest. They want to believe you're an expert but a modest expert. Collaborative effort framing builds trust and justifies price."
  domains: ["copywriting", "trust-building", "authority-positioning"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-599"
  title: "Text Sales Pages Are Not Dead"
  description: "Text-based sales pages are not dead. They serve as modality shifters â€” moving the reader from reading-and-hearing to purely reading to imaginative reading. They should be used as alternate landing pages and exit pages to extend the life of VSLs."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-600"
  title: "Real Scarcity Beats False Scarcity For Sleeping At Night"
  description: "Real scarcity beats the hell out of false scarcity every day when it comes to sleeping better at night. As far as conversions, both work. But real scarcity with clever framing is the ethical path."
  domains: ["copywriting", "scarcity", "ethics", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-601"
  title: "Capture The Avatar's Feeling And You Can Write Any Copy"
  description: "If you captured the feeling of the character you're writing to, you could write copy just like this. You can do this in every single industry, every single product. Write as if talking to them â€” their lingo, what they would say, what they would think."
  domains: ["copywriting", "avatar-strategy", "market-research"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-602"
  title: "The Info Apocalypse Is Coming"
  description: "The sheer volume of information received in a day places email marketing right on the mark for social extinction â€” unless you employ The Trifecta: Passion, Personality, And Persuasion. The only Internet marketers who will survive are those with the strongest voices."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "marketing-philosophy", "business-survival"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-603"
  title: "You Are Not Your List â€” You Are Your Message"
  description: "Your list is your gold, but the big-ass unbreakable safe that houses that gold is The Trifecta. When you become not just the messenger but the message itself, raving fans will follow and reward you handsomely."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "personal-branding", "marketing-philosophy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-604"
  title: "Fear Avoidance Drives 75% Of Decisions"
  description: "We will do approximately 75% more to avoid potential pain than to gain potential pleasure. Successful marketing and email has a strong fear-based modality. The monkey mind responds more to avoidance of fear than possibility of pleasure."
  domains: ["sales-psychology", "buyer-behavior", "neuromarketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-605"
  title: "Compel Rather Than Convince"
  description: "Most marketers make the mistake of trying to convince rather than attempting to compel. The Samurai knows how to compel fans into accepting actions they already hold as congruent to their greatest core values."
  domains: ["persuasion", "copywriting", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-606"
  title: "Think Fans Not Customers"
  description: "Think in terms of fans rather than customers, list segments, or demographics. Fans will stick with you, pay well for passionate delivery, and do a lot of your selling via word-of-mouth. Create marketing that attracts your ideal fan."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "relationship-marketing", "branding"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-607"
  title: "HTML Templates Are Satan's Sex Toys"
  description: "Using HTML templates to look like a 'real' company is four steps beyond stupid. Make your emails look like ones you get from your best friends â€” plain old text. The response rate goes up and tech will appreciate your sensitivity to bandwidth."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "email-design", "deliverability"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-608"
  title: "Boldness Gains Fans â€” For Every 1 Offended You Gain 2"
  description: "For every one person you offend, you will gain two or more loyal fans. When you're bold, fans respond. When you cower in fear, fans flee. Being afraid to offend is worse than offending â€” censoring yourself is a step toward becoming an Internet coward."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "personal-branding", "copywriting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-609"
  title: "Sell What You Would Buy Yourself"
  description: "Think of your service to humanity as selling others what you would buy yourself, and you'll be doing the world a favor. Honest is the operative word. Turn down offers you cannot stand behind."
  domains: ["marketing-ethics", "business-philosophy", "affiliate-marketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-610"
  title: "VSL vs Sales Page Are Different Frameworks"
  description: "A video sales letter is a rapport-based, voice-temporal format for selling. When speaking vs reading, you're in different frameworks. Reading allows more freedom. Story is essential in VSL but optional in text sales pages. You cannot cut-and-paste between them."
  domains: ["copywriting", "vsl-creation", "sales-pages"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-611"
  title: "Inaction Must Be Incongruent With Their Values"
  description: "Drive the sale by making inaction incongruent with everything they've already admitted they believe. Like Jack Nicholson in A Few Good Men â€” if they have any ounce of integrity, they must take action. Appeal to their core values."
  domains: ["sales-psychology", "persuasion", "closing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-612"
  title: "Writing If It Were Easy We Wouldn't Exist"
  description: "If writing a sales page or video sales letter was easy, we wouldn't exist. What I've done is make it as easy as humanly possible through formulas and templates."
  domains: ["copywriting", "skill-development", "business-philosophy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-613"
  title: "Nobody Cares About Your Product"
  description: "No one gives a rat's ass about your product. Not even your mom. People only care about the benefits your product gives them â€” the pain it relieves, the gratification it delivers, the responsibility they feel, the perceived status it brings."
  domains: ["copywriting", "marketing-philosophy", "benefits-focus"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-614"
  title: "NLP Anchoring In Copywriting"
  description: "In copywriting, NLP is used as a verbal tool to create anchors to predetermined values. You anchor what the fan already cares about with the action you want to compel them to consider. Smart phones amplify these anchors."
  domains: ["copywriting", "nlp", "neuromarketing", "email-marketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-615"
  title: "Swipe Copy Will Kill Your Soul"
  description: "When you send the same copy as everyone else, it screams 'I have not one unique take on this topic.' Swipe copy's days are numbered. The fan-composed meteor is about to crash. Use swipe for ideas only â€” always make it 'you'."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "copywriting", "marketing-ethics"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-616"
  title: "Redundancy Is A Copywriting Weapon"
  description: "The average person has to see the ad 7-9 times before buying. Repeating certain themes and messages throughout copy is not just okay â€” it's necessary. Use redundancy and homonyms deliberately."
  domains: ["copywriting", "repetition", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-617"
  title: "Simple And Simply Are Power Words"
  description: "Count how many times 'simple' and 'simply' appear in effective sales pages â€” often 29-30 times in a short page. The word 'you' appears 249+ times. These repetitions are not accidental â€” they're purposeful pattern builders."
  domains: ["copywriting", "word-choice", "persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-618"
  title: "Words Under The Radar Move Sales"
  description: "I purposely choose words with subliminal connotations. 'Peer' sounds like what it describes â€” peering creates rapport. 'Buy now' misspelled as 'by now' plants purchase commands. These subliminal NLP tactics are not accidental."
  domains: ["copywriting", "nlp", "sales-psychology", "subliminal-persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-619"
  title: "Transparency Builds More Trust Than Perfection"
  description: "Saying 'sure, you can find 80% of this on Google if you spend 100 hours' is totally transparent, but it also makes them feel really stupid for making any other decision than to buy. Transparency is a trust builder that outperforms hiding limitations."
  domains: ["copywriting", "trust-building", "pricing-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-620"
  title: "Write To One Avatar With Picture On Monitor"
  description: "Put a picture of your avatar on your computer monitor before you write a single word. Then write to that one person. Never think you're composing to the masses. This single technique will almost immediately make your emails more personal and impactful."
  domains: ["copywriting", "email-marketing", "avatar-strategy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-621"
  title: "Email Triple F Factors â€” Fast Fun Fingertips"
  description: "Three reasons to love email marketing: Fast (fastest way to reach largest audience), Fun (crafting clever messages that feel personal), and Fingertips (seven minutes from earning a paycheck wherever your laptop is)."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "business-philosophy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "FIL-JB-622"
  title: "Strong Copy Stands On Its Own"
  description: "If you need exclamation points to make your point, your point isn't strong to begin with. 'Just Do It.' vs 'Just Do It!!' â€” the former is powerful, the latter makes you gag. Strong copy needs no frills, no all-caps, no bolded words."
  domains: ["copywriting", "writing-craft", "email-marketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"
```

## L2_MENTAL_MODELS (MM-JB-586 to MM-JB-612) â€” 27 elements

```yaml
# L2_MENTAL_MODELS â€” 5MSLF Source

- id: "MM-JB-586"
  title: "Duality Decision Model"
  description: "Customers are in a state of indecision driven by cognitive dissonance â€” 'I know I should but I shouldn't.' When you speak in duality (pros AND cons), you mirror their internal dialogue and resolve cognitive dissonance toward action."
  domains: ["sales-psychology", "copywriting", "persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-587"
  title: "Modality Shifting Framework"
  description: "People process content through different modalities: reading+hearing â†’ reading â†’ imaginative reading â†’ watching. Using text sales pages as alternate landing pages or exit pages creates modality shifts that extend VSL life and capture non-video buyers."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-588"
  title: "Lizard Brain vs Prefrontal Cortex Model"
  description: "The monkey mind (limbic system) controls virtually every buying decision. The prefrontal cortex then rationalizes with logic. You must speak to the lizard brain (emotion) first, then provide rational justification. 'You made a wise decision' on upsell pages validates the rational brain."
  domains: ["neuromarketing", "sales-psychology", "copywriting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-589"
  title: "Negative-To-Positive Duality Sequence"
  description: "Structure copy as negative-to-positive duality. Example: 'Here's why you'd be stupid not to take it' (negative/values) followed by 'Here's a better choice' (positive/future pace). This mirrors how humans process decisions internally."
  domains: ["copywriting", "persuasion", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-590"
  title: "The Blame Removal Model"
  description: "Remove blame from the prospect and place it on external factors. 'Today's exercise programs are way too long and they are KEEPING YOU from the body you want.' The blame is no longer on them â€” it's the programs they've been following."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-psychology", "rapport-building"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-591"
  title: "Future Pacing As Present Tense"
  description: "Instead of 'you will feel' (future tense), use present tense statements like 'you remain in total control.' This future-paces the reader into already experiencing the benefit. 'Imagine this day with me' creates association and present-state anchoring."
  domains: ["copywriting", "nlp", "persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-592"
  title: "Article Page As Psychological Pre-Frame"
  description: "An article page before the sales page allows an all-positive headline on the sales page because the negatives were covered in the article. Without the article pre-frame, all-positive headlines underperform. The article sets the negative context."
  domains: ["copywriting", "funnel-architecture", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-593"
  title: "The Give vs Buy Framing"
  description: "'There are some people we do not want to give a copy of our book to' â€” using 'give' instead of 'buy' completely reframes the transaction. The prospect feels they're being given something valuable, not sold something."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-psychology", "framing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-594"
  title: "Set Theory Scarcity Model"
  description: "Using 'a few copies' leverages set theory â€” 'a few' relative to what? If there are a million potential buyers, 100,000 is 'a few.' This creates borderline ethical scarcity that's technically true. PG-13 ethics."
  domains: ["copywriting", "scarcity", "pricing-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-595"
  title: "The Parting Gift Close Model"
  description: "After all persuasion: 'If you don't take advantage of this offer, we have a parting gift for you â€” a dose of reality.' Then paint the nightmare of inaction. Followed by 'Sure that's not much of a gift, but we believe in tough love.' Devastating close."
  domains: ["copywriting", "closing", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-596"
  title: "PS Encapsulation Strategy"
  description: "The PS should encapsulate the entire sales page in 5 lines because people scroll to the bottom first. Use the AVOID-ENJOY-HOW TO-WHAT IF formula. Reference a specific paragraph to drive re-reads of the sales page."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-597"
  title: "Control As Core Value For Women Over 40"
  description: "Security and control are huge factors for women over 40. Hormones changing, bodies feeling out of control. 'You are in total control' is an incredibly powerful statement that cannot lose â€” fit women agree, struggling women yearn for it."
  domains: ["copywriting", "avatar-psychology", "market-research"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-598"
  title: "The 'Today' Anchoring Model"
  description: "Starting with 'Today's exercise programs...' anchors action in the present. Each small word choice is a match â€” one doesn't matter, but stack enough matches and you reach the moon. 'Today' creates urgency subconsciously."
  domains: ["copywriting", "nlp", "word-choice"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-599"
  title: "Nostalgia Marketing For Older Demographics"
  description: "For audiences over 40, use 'old school' framing. They resent modern softness and long for the days of their youth. 'Today's exercise programs are way too long' â€” they immediately think something bad, then hear something good. Duality meets nostalgia."
  domains: ["copywriting", "avatar-psychology", "demographic-targeting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-600"
  title: "Avoidance vs Enjoyment Modality"
  description: "Two modalities in marketing: Avoidance (fear-based, stronger â€” 'Read this or die') and Enjoyment (pleasure-based, still useful â€” 'Read this and cash in'). The monkey mind prefers avoidance. Use both, but lead with avoidance."
  domains: ["sales-psychology", "copywriting", "neuromarketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-601"
  title: "The Trifecta Safe House Model"
  description: "The Trifecta â€” Passion, Personality, And Persuasion â€” is the ultimate survival kit for the Info Apocalypse. Passion from your fans, not just you. Personality as your unique voice. Persuasion as the ethical Samurai sword. All three create the unbreakable safe."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "personal-branding", "marketing-strategy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-602"
  title: "Making Negatives Positive Model"
  description: "Turn every product weakness into a strength: 'It's not professionally published' â†’ no overhead passed to customer. 'It's only 16 pages' â†’ how many pages would a treasure map need? 'I didn't edit it' â†’ you care about information, not covers."
  domains: ["copywriting", "objection-handling", "pricing-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-603"
  title: "Commitment Ladder Through Clicks"
  description: "Send short email in parts requiring clicks to continue. Clicking trains commitment. Small commitments add up to larger ones â€” the sale. Each click is a micro-yes that primes the monkey brain for the final buy decision."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "persuasion", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-604"
  title: "The Open Loop Teaching Method"
  description: "Open loops throughout content (mentioning something you'll cover later) keep attention and create curiosity. 'I'll cover them shortly. (That was called opening a loop. You'll use that a lot.)' Loops drive completion and engagement."
  domains: ["copywriting", "content-creation", "engagement"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-605"
  title: "Rapport Through Shared Experience"
  description: "Create experiences that you and the reader both share in the email. Instead of talking about a product, tell a shared story. 'Andrea tried this coffee â€” it changed everything.' Count the 'you' words and notice the conversation feel."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "rapport-building", "storytelling"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-606"
  title: "Price Anchoring Through Discovery Page"
  description: "For expensive products, don't put the price on the page. Let them click to discover it on a checkout page with a 30-second video: 'Hey, I almost forgot to tell you, it's only $17, and here's why.' Creates curiosity and engagement."
  domains: ["pricing-psychology", "funnel-architecture", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-607"
  title: "Three Persona Archetypes For Fan Building"
  description: "People love groundbreakers, champions, and underdogs. One of those three probably fits you. Select one and write under that persona with all honesty and as few words as needed. Verbosity is cool in books but death for most emails."
  domains: ["personal-branding", "email-marketing", "voice-development"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-608"
  title: "Triple-F Email Value Proposition"
  description: "Email marketing succeeds because of Fast (largest audience quickly), Fun (crafting messages that feel personal), and Fingertips (7 minutes from paycheck anywhere). This framework justifies email over other channels."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "marketing-strategy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-609"
  title: "Stern Points Phenomenon"
  description: "Going over-the-top offensive can work if done so extremely it gains 'Stern Points' â€” people buy because the offense itself sounds more believable than polished marketing. The Rich Jerk example: 'I'm rich, you're not, and I'm better than you.' Extreme personality wins."
  domains: ["copywriting", "personal-branding", "provocation-marketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-610"
  title: "Swipe Copy Modification Protocol"
  description: "Three questions before sending swipe: 'Does this sound like me?' 'How would I respond to this?' 'How can I make it more personal?' Spending 30 minutes rewriting increases sales 83% of the time and decreases opt-outs."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "copywriting", "affiliate-marketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-611"
  title: "Mobile Device Emotional Amplifier"
  description: "People have more affinity to their cell phones than laptops. Phones serve as NLP anchors to everything they care about most. Email checked on a phone is processed through a more personal, emotional lens. Write with mobile context in mind."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "mobile-marketing", "neuromarketing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "MM-JB-612"
  title: "Modality Switching In Funnels"
  description: "For tripwire ($7-17) followed by upsells: text page for the tripwire, VSL for the first upsell, then text-based for subsequent upsells. Switch modalities on purpose. Never do two VSLs in a row. Always test."
  domains: ["funnel-architecture", "conversion-optimization", "copywriting"]
  source: "5MSLF"
```

## L3_HEURISTICS (HEUR-JB-626 to HEUR-JB-672) â€” 47 elements

```yaml
# L3_HEURISTICS â€” 5MSLF Source

- id: "HEUR-JB-626"
  title: "3-Line Paragraph Maximum Rule"
  description: "Sales page paragraphs should be no more than 3 lines. Some are 2. If there's a narrow image, you can use 5-6 lines (newspaper print). But across the full page, 3 lines is the maximum. Tested and proven."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "formatting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-627"
  title: "75% Of Openings Should Have 'You'"
  description: "75% of good sales letter introductions have the word 'you' in the opening paragraph a lot. Jon broke the record with Old School New Body â€” 9 'you' words in the first paragraph. 249 'you' words in the entire page."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "word-choice"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-628"
  title: "5-Paragraph Rapport Intro"
  description: "The rapport intro should be about 5 paragraphs, heavy on the word 'you'. This builds rapport before any selling begins. Optional 'Fact' style intro can replace this."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "rapport-building"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-629"
  title: "4-7 Paragraphs For 'What It Isn't' Section"
  description: "The 'What It Isn't' section should be 4-7 paragraphs plus a paragraph starting with the underlined command 'Listen.' This establishes what you're NOT before presenting what you ARE."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "structure"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-630"
  title: "Use 2 Of The 4 E's"
  description: "The 4 E's for hook proof: Engagers (testimonials), Evidence (research), Experience (personal), Expertise (authority). Usually use 2 of 4 for standard pages; use all 4 for longer pages."
  domains: ["copywriting", "credibility-building", "sales-pages"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-631"
  title: "5-8 Paragraphs Comparison Copy"
  description: "The 'Us vs Them' section should be 5-8 paragraphs of comparison copy. Include 1 'Did you know' informative fact. You're comparing your answer to all alternatives, preemptively answering objections."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "competitive-positioning"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-632"
  title: "3-5 Paragraphs For Description Of Experience"
  description: "The 'How You Will Feel' section should be 3-5 paragraphs describing the emotional experience after purchase. Use future pacing in present tense."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "future-pacing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-633"
  title: "1-3 Paragraphs Product Intro Only"
  description: "Keep the product introduction to only 1-3 paragraphs. People aren't buying the product â€” they're buying the emotions. Going too much into features is 'all pudding' and hurts conversions."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "product-description"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-634"
  title: "$197 And Under = Sales Page Format"
  description: "The nightmare story and 'don't try this alone' format works fantastic for medium-priced products â€” anything $197 and down. For $5,000+ products, totally different copy â€” take an application and call them."
  domains: ["copywriting", "pricing-psychology", "sales-pages"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-635"
  title: "3-5 Paragraphs Scarcity Copy"
  description: "The scarcity section should be 3-5 paragraphs with compelling reasons to act now. Real scarcity always preferred. Can say 'I may increase the price' if you really think you may."
  domains: ["copywriting", "scarcity", "urgency"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-636"
  title: "5-7 Paragraphs Values Incongruence Close"
  description: "The 'You'd Be Stupid Not To Take It' section should be 5-7 paragraphs explaining why inaction is incongruent with their stated values. This is the hammer that drives the nail through."
  domains: ["copywriting", "closing", "persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-637"
  title: "PS Recap In 5 Lines Maximum"
  description: "The PPS should encapsulate the entire sales page in only about 5 lines. Place it right above the order button because people scroll to the bottom first. Use format: Problem â†’ Solution â†’ How To Get It."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "closing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-638"
  title: "PS AVOID-ENJOY-HOW TO-WHAT IF Formula"
  description: "The PS strategy: AVOID (what to avoid), ENJOY (benefits), HOW TO (the method), WHAT IF (imagine the outcome). Reference a specific paragraph number to drive re-reads. Fits in 2-3 lines."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "closing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-639"
  title: "50-50 Split Text vs VSL"
  description: "Run a 50-50 split between text sales page and VSL. Text page picks up people who didn't buy from VSL. Conversions automatically go up. Unless text page conversions are 3X lower than VSL."
  domains: ["conversion-optimization", "split-testing", "funnel-architecture"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-640"
  title: "9 Exposures Before Purchase"
  description: "The average person has to see the ad 9 times before they buy (maybe 7). This justifies repeating certain themes throughout copy. Repetition is okay and necessary."
  domains: ["copywriting", "marketing-psychology", "repetition"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-641"
  title: "Simple/Simply 29+ Times Per Page"
  description: "The word 'simple' and 'simply' appeared 29-30 times in the Old School New Body sales page. This repetition is intentional and builds the perception of ease throughout the copy."
  domains: ["copywriting", "word-choice", "persuasion"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-642"
  title: "2 Negatives Followed By Positive In Did You Know"
  description: "Structure 'Did you know' paragraphs as: negative fact, negative fact, positive fact. This creates tension-release that mirrors duality marketing principles."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "structure"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-643"
  title: "Start Bullets With Numbers Or Verbs"
  description: "When writing benefits in bullet form, always start with a number or a verb. '13 ways to...' or 'Discover how...' Bullets in text sales pages are different from VSL bullets."
  domains: ["copywriting", "bullet-writing", "formatting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-644"
  title: "3-5 Nightmare Story Paragraphs"
  description: "The 'Don't Try This Alone' nightmare story should be 3-5 paragraphs. Brief and impactful. One customer tried alone for 4 years â€” finally reached out for help. Plus risk of misinformation."
  domains: ["copywriting", "storytelling", "risk-reversal"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-645"
  title: "Article Page 5 Sound-Bite Points With Commands"
  description: "Article page must have: short headline + byline, 'Did you know' copy (3-7 paragraphs), sub-headline acting as headline, 3-5 sound-bite points starting with commands/verbs (Forget, Stop, Avoid, Work), optional warning, 'What do I do next?' link."
  domains: ["copywriting", "article-pages", "lead-generation"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-646"
  title: "Remember vs Don't Forget Rule"
  description: "'Remember' is much more powerful than 'don't forget.' Your brain doesn't hear the word 'don't.' 'Remember' is a positive command that can be followed. Only use 'don't forget' when in a hurry."
  domains: ["copywriting", "nlp", "word-choice"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-647"
  title: "147 Emails Per Day Threshold"
  description: "Average person receives 147 emails per day, deletes nearly 50% before reading past subject line. Over 2.6 hours per day spent on email. Each email takes 3 seconds to process. 182 hours per year deleting unwanted emails."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "deliverability", "market-reality"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-648"
  title: "Asking First Name Costs 10% Opt-Ins But Worth It"
  description: "Asking for first name decreases opt-ins by about 10%. However, having the first name drastically increases personality factor and rapport. Studies show higher lifetime value per customer. Take the 10% hit."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "opt-in-optimization", "customer-ltv"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-649"
  title: "8th Grade Reading Level Maximum"
  description: "Keep sales copy at an 8th grade reading level or lower. Half your audience cannot read over that level. If a dictionary is required, your copy sucks. Let an average 8th grader read it to test."
  domains: ["copywriting", "readability", "audience-accessibility"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-650"
  title: "10-15 Words Per Sentence In Emails"
  description: "Keep email sentences to 10-15 words for maximum readability and engagement. This is the 'Samurai 10-Word Trick' for emails."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "copywriting", "readability"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-651"
  title: "More Body Copy Than URLs In Email"
  description: "Always have more body copy text than URL/link text in emails. If you don't, spam filters flag it. More links than copy = spam folder destination."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "deliverability", "email-structure"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-652"
  title: "Swipe Rewrite Boosts Sales 83% Of The Time"
  description: "Spending 30 minutes personalizing swipe copy increases sales and conversions 83% of the time and decreases opt-outs. Ask: 'Does this sound like me?' 'How would I respond?' 'How can I make it more personal?'"
  domains: ["email-marketing", "affiliate-marketing", "copywriting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-653"
  title: "5 Email Promotions Per Week Was Too Many"
  description: "Jon was sending 5 promotions a week to fans, many swipe copy, and grew careless. This was too many. Quality and voice must be maintained â€” frequency without personality burns lists."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "list-management", "email-frequency"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-654"
  title: "Subject Lines Short Enough For Phone Preview"
  description: "Subject lines should be short enough to be fully readable within the smart phone email preview. Most email is now checked on phones â€” if they can't read the full subject, open rates drop."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "mobile-marketing", "subject-lines"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-655"
  title: "No Hard Returns In Email â€” Mobile Breaks Them"
  description: "Never use hard returns in emails to keep paragraphs short. Smart phones tear up hard-return paragraphs into ransom note formatting. Use plain text without hard returns for cross-device compatibility."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "mobile-marketing", "formatting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-656"
  title: "3-7 Paragraphs Hook Description With 4 E's"
  description: "Description of the hook should be 3-7 paragraphs plus 4 E's proof elements. This is where the trademark/hook is explained and validated with social proof."
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "hook-development"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-657"
  title: "3-5 Paragraphs Collaborative Effort Copy"
  description: "The 'Can't Take All Credit' section should be 3-5 paragraphs about team effort, standing on shoulders of giants. Immediately followed by 'However, don't try this alone.'"
  domains: ["copywriting", "sales-pages", "humility-positioning"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-658"
  title: "3-7 Paragraphs Why It's Affordable"
  description: "The 'Why It's Just $X' section should be 3-7 paragraphs making negative objections positive. Cover: not professionally published, not very long, no shipping, treasure map analogy."
  domains: ["copywriting", "pricing-psychology", "objection-handling"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-659"
  title: "Use 'But' Only For Objections"
  description: "Avoid the word 'but' throughout sales copy. The only place Jon likes to use it is in the objections section: 'But I'm 69 years old...' Prefer 'What if I'm...' or 'What about...' instead."
  domains: ["copywriting", "word-choice", "objection-handling"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-660"
  title: "Respond To Objections With Positive Escalation"
  description: "Start objection responses with escalating positive words: 'Great.' 'Ideal time.' 'Then you really need it.' 'Terrific.' This is complete duality â€” addressing the negative with overwhelming positivity."
  domains: ["copywriting", "objection-handling", "sales-psychology"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-661"
  title: "Sell Bonuses â€” Don't List Them"
  description: "Work on selling your bonuses with compelling bullet copy. Start each bonus bullet with: amazing, secret, specific, how. Simple but powerful. Bonus copy that's too long can work against you."
  domains: ["copywriting", "offer-structure", "bonuses"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-662"
  title: "Digital Product Graphic Must Say Digital"
  description: "Always include 'All material is digital â€” that means you can start today' with a clear graphic. Many customers complain they didn't know it was digital. This solves refunds and confusion."
  domains: ["copywriting", "product-presentation", "customer-experience"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-663"
  title: "Tripwire Text Then VSL Upsell"
  description: "For inexpensive tripwires ($7-17) followed by 3 upsells: text page for tripwire, VSL for first upsell, text-based for subsequent upsells. Switch modalities on purpose to re-engage."
  domains: ["funnel-architecture", "conversion-optimization", "pricing"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-664"
  title: "P90X Sound-Alike Hook Strategy"
  description: "F4X worked as a hook because it sounds like P90X. People linked them subconsciously. When creating hooks/trademarks, consider phonetic associations with popular brands. Sometimes imperfect hooks stick because of association."
  domains: ["hook-development", "branding", "naming-strategy"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-665"
  title: "Once Weekly Personal CEO Email From Brand"
  description: "If using HTML templates, send one text-based personal email per week from 'The Desk Of [CEO Name].' Tell a story about your week, a customer, something relevant. Treat fans as people once a week. Fortune 500 companies who adopted this reaped serious benefits."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "personal-branding", "corporate-email"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-666"
  title: "Empty First Name Field Spacing Trick"
  description: "To minimize damage from blank first name fields: 'Hi ,' with a space before comma, and 'so much ?' with space before question mark. The missing name becomes almost unnoticeable. Remove 'Friend' as default â€” friends know your name."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "personalization", "technical-tips"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-667"
  title: "Either-Or Subject Lines Drive Opens"
  description: "Asking either-or questions in subject lines works great: 'Do you prefer coffee or water?' Gets opens even from people who dislike one option because they identify with the other."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "subject-lines", "open-rates"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-668"
  title: "Over 400 'You' Words In One Sales Letter"
  description: "In one sales letter, Jon used 'you' over 400 times, often up to 10 times in a single paragraph. Purposely looked for ways to cram 'you' into places it didn't belong. No one noticed except the monkey mind."
  domains: ["copywriting", "word-choice", "rapport-building"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-669"
  title: "Avoid Trite Phrases And Emoticons"
  description: "Avoid: emoticons, excessive smiley faces, phrases like 'This is the first day of the rest of your life' or 'The greatest journey begins with a single step.' Trite sucks. Avoid clichÃ©s 90% of the time."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "copywriting", "writing-craft"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-670"
  title: "Use Exclamation Points Only When Unexpected"
  description: "Rarely use exclamation points. Use them only when they're not expected. Some of the best copy contained no frills â€” no '!', no all-caps, no bolded words save one perhaps."
  domains: ["copywriting", "punctuation", "writing-craft"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-671"
  title: "All-Caps Trigger Spam Filters"
  description: "All-caps trigger spam filters, look desperate, create ransom note feel. If used at all, limit to one strategic place. Clever words sit on their own without fancy pedestals."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "deliverability", "formatting"]
  source: "5MSLF"

- id: "HEUR-JB-672"
  title: "Fewer Pics Compressed Like Hell"
  description: "Fewer pictures in emails, compressed heavily when used. Respect bandwidth. Three pictures of you looking fabulous creates jealousy not rapport. Keep pics timely and relevant to events or celebrities in your field."
  domains: ["email-marketing", "email-design", "bandwidth"]
  source: "5MSLF"
```

```yaml
- id: "FW-JB-646"
  title: "Hook Formula (Simple Version)"
  description: "4-quadrant system: Right side (hooks) = 3 Primary Benefits + 3 Primary Fears. Left side (stories/headlines) = 3 Villains + 3 Heroes. Key question for hooks: 'If EVERYONE had [benefit/fear], what would the world look like?' Combine left + right like dominoes for headlines."
  domains: ["hook-creation", "headlines", "brainstorming"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-647"
  title: "3X Hook Formula (Meta-Values Matrix)"
  description: "Structured hook development: Map Meta-Values (Health, Energy, Love, Family, Safety) â†’ Success Looks Like â†’ Present Looks Like â†’ Role Model Is â†’ Magic Pill â†’ 'That Is As If' â†’ 'Other Than You, Who?' (villains) â†’ 'If Everyone Had Problem' (apocalyptic hook)."
  domains: ["hook-creation", "copywriting", "positioning"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-648"
  title: "Three Samurai Headline Formulas"
  description: "Formula 1: Power Word + Hook/Primary Benefit + (Optional Adjective) + Verb + Target + Promise. Formula 2: Number + Adjective + Power Word + Product/Primary Benefit + Verb + Target + Promise. Formula 3: Power Word + Descriptor + Product Feature + Power Word + Verb + Primary Benefit + While/Withouts."
  domains: ["headlines", "copywriting", "formulas"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-649"
  title: "Enhanced Rapport Formula"
  description: "3-phase pre-nightmare story technique: Phase 1 'BACK IN THE DAY' â€” I'm a lot like you + modest intro + early dabbling + answer 3 objections within story. Phase 2 'LIFE WAS GOOD' â€” Started seeing results + tease one thing that showed promise. Phase 3 'THEN IT HAPPENED' â€” Tragic event that sends into downward spiral â†’ feeds into standard nightmare story."
  domains: ["rapport-building", "storytelling", "copywriting"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-650"
  title: "Triple Guarantee Architecture"
  description: "Three-layer guarantee: 1) If you don't drop expected weight in 60 days, 2) If you don't lose at least 10% in first 30 days, 3) Even if you just don't like the font or design. Leave them NO excuses. The more absurd the third guarantee, the more confident you appear."
  domains: ["guarantees", "risk-reversal", "closing"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-651"
  title: "Price Reveal Sequence"
  description: "Full pricing sequence: 1) Retail value ($97 â€” have it on site at this price), 2) Add bonuses with individual values, 3) Total if bought separately ($200+), 4) 'Just for watching' better deal, 5) Cost of loss (10-100x more in alternatives), 6) 'That isn't what I want for you,' 7) Cause/reason for discount, 8) Triple drop ($217 â†’ half â†’ $67 â†’ actual $47), 9) 'One payment of only.'"
  domains: ["pricing-psychology", "closing", "vsl-creation"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-652"
  title: "Nightmare Story Architecture"
  description: "Structure: 1) Specific date/time/conditions ('Tuesday, November 6th, 1992. It was raining.'), 2) Companion/setting ('My buddy Steve'), 3) Build through vivid, ridiculous, embarrassing details, 4) Lowest point revelation, 5) 'It wasn't even THAT that changed things...' (escalate), 6) Declaration of Independence ('I swore I would find the solution or die trying'), 7) Transition to hero's return."
  domains: ["storytelling", "copywriting", "rapport-building"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-653"
  title: "Cause-Based Pricing Justification"
  description: "Create a real cause tied to your product. Example: '10 Million Pounds Down' campaign. Use the cause to justify lower pricing: 'I don't want finances to stand in your way of joining the cause.' People feel guilty NOT joining a cause, adding social pressure to buy."
  domains: ["pricing-psychology", "cause-marketing", "copywriting"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-654"
  title: "Tip Structure With Branded Closer"
  description: "3-tip structure: Tip 1 (AVOID): What to avoid and why (3-7 slides). Tip 2 (ENJOY): How to enjoy the process (reframe objections). Tip 3 (HOW-TO): The branded, trademarked technique â€” this is your USP. Open a massive loop on tip 3, deliver the concept but withhold the implementation (that's in the product)."
  domains: ["content-structure", "vsl-creation", "copywriting"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-655"
  title: "The Two Choices Close"
  description: "Present exactly two choices: Choice 1: Take information and try to go it ALONE (emphasize 'alone'). Choice 2: Let me do the work for you â€” join thousands of success stories. Frame it so Choice 2 is the only rational decision."
  domains: ["closing", "copywriting", "decision-architecture"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-656"
  title: "Pain-Pleasure Close Sequence"
  description: "After CTA: 1) Remind of long struggle (pain), 2) Cost of continued inaction (more pain), 3) YET all of this can change THE MOMENT you begin (present tense pleasure), 4) Future pace â€” 'You see the weight coming off... you are experiencing the energy,' 5) 'It's true â€” not entirely your fault,' 6) 'However, today marks YOUR day to take responsibility.'"
  domains: ["closing", "future-pacing", "copywriting"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-657"
  title: "Action Results Close Framework"
  description: "Post-purchase instruction sequence: 1) Tell them exactly what to download first, 2) 'Flip to page 4 to begin your assessment,' 3) Restate ALL books and bonuses, 4) 'You have nothing to lose,' 5) Three reasons to buy NOW (price increase, bonus scarcity, 'if you don't act now you may never act')."
  domains: ["closing", "post-purchase", "vsl-creation"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-658"
  title: "Hero Study Credibility Framework"
  description: "Instead of claiming personal genius, say: 'This is the result of 10 years of studying the most successful long-term case studies ever.' Position yourself as the curator of OTHER people's success. 'We analyzed 15,000 people and they all came to the same conclusion.'"
  domains: ["credibility", "copywriting", "positioning"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-659"
  title: "Upsell Psychology Framework"
  description: "Four upsell angles: 1) FASTER â€” 'Want faster results?', 2) DONE WITH YOU â€” 'Let us help you,' 3) DONE FOR YOU â€” 'Let us do it for you,' 4) MORE OF IT â€” 'More recipes/modules.' Never COMPETE with initial product. Always position as OPTIONAL acceleration."
  domains: ["upsells", "funnel-strategy", "offers"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-660"
  title: "Third-Person Rapport Story"
  description: "For experts/doctors who can't self-deprecate: Tell Sally's story. 'I want to tell you about one of my patients â€” her name is Sally. A lot of people can really relate to Sally's story.' Build rapport through a relatable third person while positioning the expert as the hero who saved them."
  domains: ["rapport-building", "storytelling", "positioning"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-661"
  title: "Customer Survey To Word Cloud Pipeline"
  description: "Complete data pipeline: 1) Send 5-question survey, 2) Separate answers by question, 3) Paste each question's answers into word cloud, 4) Extract top 5 words per question, 5) Map: Q1=fears, Q2=knowledge needs, Q3=product naming, Q4=visual promises, Q5=emotional hooks. Use throughout entire VSL."
  domains: ["market-research", "data-analysis", "copywriting"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-662"
  title: "VSL Length Decision Framework"
  description: "Main VSL: Full 5-step (45-90 minutes for info products). Upsell 1: 5-minute formula + 1-min personal bump. Upsell 2: 12-18 minute VSL. Lead magnet: 1-5 minute VSL. No-brainer supplement upsell: Under 5 minutes. Service/consulting: Use as lead qualification tool."
  domains: ["vsl-creation", "funnel-strategy", "content-structure"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-663"
  title: "Quiz-To-Sale Funnel Structure"
  description: "Modern funnel: Facebook Ad â†’ Quiz (micro-commitments via yes/no answers) â†’ VSL/Sales Page. Quizzes create engagement and qualify prospects without requiring a four-video launch sequence. Faster path to sale than traditional product launches."
  domains: ["funnel-strategy", "lead-generation", "conversion-optimization"]
  source: "VSLFT18"

- id: "FW-JB-664"
  title: "Works-Even-If Obstacle Elimination"
  description: "Systematic objection crusher: List every reason someone wouldn't buy ('I've tried before,' 'I'm too old,' 'I'm a beginner'). Create one slide per obstacle: 'Works even if [obstacle].' End with 'Or... if you think you've tried everything.' Cover beginners, intermediate, AND advanced."
  domains: ["objection-handling", "copywriting", "closing"]
  source: "VSLFT18"
```

---

## OVERVIEW

VSL Fast Track 2018 is a compressed, action-oriented version of Jon Benson's VSL methodology. It focuses on the pre-VSL preparation phase (which Jon considers 80% of the work), hook creation, headline formulas, and funnel architecture including quiz funnels and upsell strategy. This is the 2018 snapshot of Jon's system before the NOWVSL 2020 expansion.

---

## SECTIONS

| # | Section | Focus |
|---|---------|-------|
| 1 | 5-Step Pre-VSL Checklist | Avatar doc, competitor analysis, mechanism, proof stack, offer wireframe |
| 2 | Hook Formula | Specific Result + Specific Timeframe + Unique Mechanism |
| 3 | 3X Hook Formula | 3 hooks (curiosity, fear, desire) tested via paid traffic |
| 4 | Enhanced Rapport Formula | Calculated vulnerability + relevant credential + specific empathy |
| 5 | Three Samurai Headline Formulas | "How To X Without Y", "The Secret of X That Y", "Warning: Don't X Until You Y" |
| 6 | Quiz-To-Sale Funnel | 5-7 question quiz that segments and pre-qualifies before VSL |
| 7 | Pre-Launch Strategy | 3-email sequence: teaser + reveal + countdown with open loops |
| 8 | Upsell & High-Ticket Architecture | Order bumps, upsell sequencing, high-ticket VSL paradox |

---

## PADROES DE PENSAMENTO

### Offer-First Reasoning

**Descricao:**
Goff consistently reasons from the offer outward. He doesn't start with "what should the copy say" â€” he starts with "does the market WANT this?" Every tactical recommendation traces back to offer appeal as the primary variable. When a funnel fails, his first question is always about the offer, never about the copy. This is the core differentiator between Goff's approach and most copywriting educators.

**Exemplos de uso:**
- When diagnosing a failing funnel, immediately asks about front-end conversion on cold traffic â€” below 1% = offer problem
- When evaluating a business opportunity, applies PaleoSweets vs PaleoPlenty mental model (400K vs 2K sales on same traffic)
- When advising a copywriter, tells them to validate the offer concept before writing a single word of copy

**Rastreabilidade:**
| chunk_id | insight_id | PATH_RAIZ | Citacao bruta |
|----------|------------|-----------|---------------|
| â€” | FIL-JG-001 | knowledge/external/sources/justin-goff/raw/ | *Awaiting Pipeline Jarvis chunk assignment* |

---

### AOV-Centric Business Thinking

**Descricao:**
Goff thinks about business growth through the lens of Average Order Value, not traffic volume. His entire Patriot Health Alliance strategy was built on this: front-end at breakeven, profit through upsells. This creates a distinctive reasoning pattern where every funnel decision is evaluated by its impact on AOV. More traffic is not the answer â€” higher AOV is the answer.

**Exemplos de uso:*

**Descricao:**
Stefan evaluates every product, offer, and piece of copy through the lens of its unique mechanism. "What's the mechanism?" is his first diagnostic question when copy isn't converting. If the mechanism is weak, nothing else matters.

**Exemplos de uso:**
- "Your copy isn't converting? What's the unique mechanism? Can you explain it in one sentence?"
- "The mechanism of the problem is as important as the mechanism of the solution."
- "Corruption breeds copy gold â€” find the hidden villain and your mechanism becomes the antidote."

{'file_path': 'C:/Users/ISMAEL_MELLO/Downloads/mega-brain-premium/agents/external/stefan-georgi/MEMORY.md', 'old_string': '### Primary (10/10 depth)

### Secondary (7-9/10 depth)

| Dominio | Descricao | Elementos |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Testing/Split Tests** | 300+ conversion minimums, statistical significance, headline testing matrix | ~12 |
| **Headlines** | Testing hierarchy, curiosity + specificity + emotion + mechanism | ~8 |
| **Offers/AOV** | Offer stack builder, stock-up scarcity, AOV Money Close, $200+ target | ~10 |
| **Email Marketing** | Subject Line Matrix (12 categories), open patterns, email structure | ~8 |

Copy Accelerator isn't about me or Justin hoarding secrets. It's about sharing everything so we all get better. I record every event, every framework, every split test result. The best copywriters I know are the most generous ones.

### AI Amplifies The Process â€” It Doesn't Replace It ^[FIL-SG-035]

AI is the biggest thing to happen to copywriting since the internet. But here's what people get wrong â€” AI doesn't replace the copywriter. AI amplifies the PROCESS. RMBC with AI is RMBC on steroids. Brief 2.0's 23 questions become Claude Project inputs. Research that took 3-5 hours now takes 30-60 minutes. But the human still drives every strategic decision.

> *"AI is the best research assistant ever invented. Claude Projects changed my entire workflow. But the human decides what's good â€” always."*

### Score Before You Fix â€” The 15 Funnel Factors ^[FIL-SG-040]

Most people jump straight to "rewrite the sales page" when conversions drop. That's ego, not strategy. The 15 Funnel Factors framework scores every element of your funnel â€” traffic, landing page, checkout, upsells, emails, everything â€” before you touch anything. Fix the lowest scores first. That's where the money is. Don't guess where the problem is â€” MEASURE it.

> *"Everyone wants to rewrite the sales page. But if your checkout is scoring a 3/10, THAT's where the money is."*

### Effective CPA Is The Only Metric That Matters ^[FIL-SG-045]

Front-end CPA is a vanity metric. What matters is Effective CPA â€” the true

<summary>Agent "Extract DNA from RMBC Method" completed</summary>
<result>I now have enough content from all the key modules to do a comprehensive extraction. Let me compile the DNA elements.

Based on my thorough reading of all 11 modules of the RMBC Method course, here is the complete DNA extraction:

```yaml
L1_PHILOSOPHIES:
  - id: "FIL-SG-030"
    name: "Consistent Quality Over Volume"
    description: "The goal is to consistently write better sales copy in less time - not to write more, but to write better and faster, which increases your effective hourly rate dramatically."
    domain: "copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-031"
    name: "Research Before Writing"
    description: "You must understand your market holistically before writing a single word. Skipping research is almost always detrimental because you end up guessing at what the market wants to hear."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-032"
    name: "Market Tells You What To Write"
    description: "The market can tell you what they want to hear and what they want you to write about, in their own language. Your job is to listen and reflect back, not to impose your ideas."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-033"
    name: "Familiar Voice Principle"
    description: "When writing copy, you must be a familiar voice to the prospect - someone they know and trust immediately because you are speaking their language and reflecting their beliefs back to them."
    domain: "copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-034"
    name: "Believability Is The Master Key"
    description: "If somebody has a pain point and they truly 100% believe that giving you money will make that pain point go away, they will buy almost every time. The entire craft is about increasing believability."
    domain: "sales-psychology"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-035"
    name: "Pay Now Or Pay Later"
    description: "Every element of copy you write in the Brief saves you time later in the actual sales letter. It is always better to invest upfront effort in the Brief than to stare at a blank screen during writing."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-036"
    name: "Selling Is Effective Communication"
    description: "Sales copy is not just about sales letters - it is really about effective communication and selling in any medium: stage pitches, presentations, webinars, one-on-one calls, and any form of persuasion."
    domain: "sales-philosophy"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-037"
    name: "Curiosity Trumps Everything"
    description: "For headlines, subject lines, and any entry point - curiosity always wins because it brings people in and makes them want to keep reading. Curiosity is the single most important element."
    domain: "copywriting-headlines"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-038"
    name: "The Lead Is Not For Education"
    description: "The purpose of the lead is to capture attention, bring them in, and get them to keep going - NOT to educate. Sharing statistics, going on boring stories, or clearing your throat in the lead is a common fatal mistake."
    domain: "copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-039"
    name: "Differentiate Both Problem And Solution"
    description: "You cannot just differentiate your solution - you must also explain why the prospect failed in the past with other solutions. The unique mechanism must cover both problem AND solution."
    domain: "copywriting-mechanism"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-040"
    name: "The Copy Is Modular"
    description: "A sales letter built with RMBC is modular - you can write sections in any order. Write the close first since it is formulaic, plug in the mechanism, then the brief content, and fill gaps. It does not have to be linear."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-041"
    name: "Big Idea As Meme"
    description: "A great big idea for a promotion works like a meme - it is short, immediately intellectually accessible, elicits an emotional response, and can be shared easily with others without needing to reference the source."
    domain: "copywriting-big-idea"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-042"
    name: "Guarantee Always Increases Conversion"
    description: "Successful marketers who have sold hundreds of millions always include guarantees because they increase conversions, reduce chargebacks, and very few people actually use them. Not having a guarantee is economically worse."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-043"
    name: "What Is Old Is New Again"
    description: "People love the idea of ancient wisdom rediscovered. Historical solutions, forgotten remedies, and old attempts at solving problems make for compelling copy because everyone believes earlier times were simpler."
    domain: "copywriting-hooks"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FIL-SG-044"
    name: "Fall From Eden Angle"
    description: "The corruption angle works across all verticals: things used to be better, then some outside force corrupted it. This narrative of a fall from a simpler time resonates universally with prospects."
    domain: "copywriting-angles"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

L2_MENTAL_MODELS:
  - id: "MM-SG-023"
    name: "The Missing 1% Model"
    description: "Position the prospect as having had 99% of the solution already - they just needed one crucial missing piece of information. This makes it easy for them to believe your product will complete the puzzle."
    domain: "copywriting-mechanism"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-024"
    name: "Effective Hourly Rate Model"
    description: "If you charge $10,000 for a sales letter and it takes 100 hours, your rate is $100/hour. If RMBC cuts that to 20 hours, your rate becomes $500/hour. Speed of quality output determines economic value."
    domain: "copywriting-business"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-025"
    name: "Headline Complex Model"
    description: "A headline is not a single line - it is a complex of pre-header, main headline, and post-header. This gives you much more real estate to incorporate curiosity, pain points, promises, specificity, and credibility."
    domain: "copywriting-headlines"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-026"
    name: "Prospect Belief Spectrum"
    description: "Online conversion rates of 1-5% mean only 1-5% believe you enough to buy. The higher you can push that believability factor through unique mechanisms and proof, the higher your conversion rate goes."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-027"
    name: "The Crossroads Close"
    description: "Present two choices: keep suffering on the current path or take action and change their life forever. This binary decision frame eliminates the paralysis of multiple options and forces a clear yes/no."
    domain: "copywriting-closing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-028"
    name: "Surface-To-Root Research Model"
    description: "Start with Amazon reviews and forums to understand market language, then go to Science Daily and news for mechanism ideas, then dig into actual studies. Go from market-level to science-level depth."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-029"
    name: "Paradoxical Question As Mechanism Setup"
    description: "A paradoxical question sets up and explains both the mechanism and the product. It creates an irresistible curiosity gap that the prospect must resolve, making them receptive to your mechanism explanation."
    domain: "copywriting-mechanism"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-030"
    name: "Big Idea vs USP Distinction"
    description: "The big idea relates to the promise of the sales copy - what captures attention and makes you engage. The USP relates to the product being sold. Big idea comes first and makes it easy to accept the USP."
    domain: "copywriting-big-idea"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-031"
    name: "Story Illustrates But Is Not The Big Idea"
    description: "The story is used to illustrate the big idea but is not itself the big idea. The mechanism is close to the big idea but lengthier. The big idea is the single transferable concept someone can quickly share."
    domain: "copywriting-big-idea"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MM-SG-032"
    name: "Contrarian Mechanism Selection"
    description: "When choosing between multiple possible mechanisms, prefer the contrarian one that challenges conventional wisdom - it creates more curiosity and differentiation than confirming what people already believe."
    domain: "copywriting-mechanism"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

L3_HEURISTICS:
  - id: "HEUR-SG-034"
    name: "12 Sales Letters Per Month Threshold"
    description: "Stefan's compensation structure incentivized writing 12 sales letters per month at $4,000 each ($48k/month) vs 4 letters at $1,000 each ($4k). This forced the systematic creation of RMBC."
    domain: "copywriting-business"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-035"
    name: "Brief Writes 20-40% Of Sales Letter"
    description: "If you do the Brief correctly by writing content in a way that can appear in the actual copy, you will have written 20-40% of your sales letter before you start the C (Copy) step."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-036"
    name: "1-5% Online Conversion Rate Benchmark"
    description: "For a $50-$100 online product, actual conversion rates are typically 1-5%. This is the percentage of people you can get to believe you enough to buy through all your copy efforts."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-037"
    name: "Close Is 1500-2000 Words"
    description: "The close section of a sales letter is typically about 1500-2000 words and is the most formulaic and templatized part. It can be swiped from competitors most directly."
    domain: "copywriting-structure"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-038"
    name: "Typical Long-Form Sales Letter Is 6000-7000 Words"
    description: "A typical long-form sales letter runs 6,000-7,000 words depending on niche, making the Brief's contribution of 1,400-2,200 words a significant percentage of the final output."
    domain: "copywriting-structure"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-039"
    name: "FAQs Increase Conversions 10-15%"
    description: "Adding FAQs to video sales letters and written sales letters increases conversions by 10-15% because people miss information, zone out, or have unanswered questions that prevent purchase."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-040"
    name: "AOV Increase Of 20-40% From Money Close"
    description: "Using the 7-step AOV Money Close to structure multi-package offers increases average order value by 20% to 40%, a very significant uplift for any offer."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-041"
    name: "Three Calls To Action In Close"
    description: "The close should contain at least three separate calls to action: the first after presenting the product and price, the second after the guarantee, and the third after the crossroads close."
    domain: "copywriting-closing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-042"
    name: "Brief Takes 2-3 Hours But Saves Days"
    description: "A properly done Brief takes 2-3 hours but produces 20-40% of the final sales letter content, making the actual copy writing phase dramatically faster."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-043"
    name: "Odd Numbers Are More Specific And Believable"
    description: "In headlines and claims, specific odd numbers like 1,425 or 22 are more believable than round numbers. Specificity signals truthfulness - '50 yards' beats 'more yards', '10 strokes' beats 'several strokes'."
    domain: "copywriting-specificity"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-044"
    name: "Use Named Specificity For People"
    description: "Always use specific names rather than generic references. 'Carmen's hopeless arthritis' is far more powerful than 'her arthritis' or 'a woman's arthritis'. Names add believability and emotional connection."
    domain: "copywriting-specificity"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-045"
    name: "Subject Line Matrix Has 15 Categories"
    description: "The Subject Line Matrix organizes email subject lines across 15 categories (columns A-O): Promise, Problem/Dimensionalized, New Cause Discovered, New Mechanism, Well-known Remedy Wrong, Taboo as Solution, Dominant Emotion, Story, Conflict/Controversy/Conspiracy, Problems of Normal Solution, People, Topical, Believability, Urgency/Scarcity, Targeted."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-046"
    name: "Product Buildup Is Only 5-10% Of Total Copy"
    description: "The product buildup and reveal section is one of the shortest original writing sections at only 5-10% of total copy length, around 500-1000 words depending on overall copy length."
    domain: "copywriting-structure"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-047"
    name: "Background Story Should Be Around 1000 Words"
    description: "A well-written background story runs about 1,000 words - roughly one-sixth to one-seventh of the total sales letter. This is a manageable chunk that can be written in the Brief phase."
    domain: "copywriting-structure"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-048"
    name: "Hint Extension For Fascinations"
    description: "Adding 'hint: it has nothing to do with X' to fascination bullets is a powerful way to extend them and add another layer of curiosity and intrigue."
    domain: "copywriting-fascinations"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-049"
    name: "Page Number References Increase Desire"
    description: "When selling info products (books, courses), adding specific page numbers to fascinations (e.g., 'see page 73') dramatically increases desire to purchase because readers want to find those specific answers."
    domain: "copywriting-fascinations"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-050"
    name: "Research Sources: Amazon, Forums, Google"
    description: "The three primary research sources are Amazon reviews (especially 1-star and 5-star), forums where the target market congregates, and Google searches. Amazon reviews provide the market's actual language."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-051"
    name: "Mechanism Research Via Science Daily"
    description: "For health offers, Science Daily is the primary source for finding unique mechanisms. Search your topic, open multiple articles, look for contrarian or new discoveries, then follow links to the actual studies for citations."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

L4_FRAMEWORKS:
  - id: "FW-SG-021"
    name: "RMBC Four-Step Method"
    description: "Research, Mechanism, Brief, Copy - a sequential four-step process where each step feeds the next to produce consistent, high-quality sales copy in less time. Most of the letter gets written before the Copy step."
    domain: "copywriting-methodology"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-022"
    name: "Research Document Framework (5 Sections)"
    description: "Research is organized into 5 sections: (1) Demographic Insights (who, attitudes, hopes/dreams, victories/failures, outside forces, prejudices, core beliefs), (2) Existing Solutions, (3) Curiosity/Historical Solutions, (4) Corruption/Fall From Eden, (5) Sources (Amazon, forums, Google)."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-023"
    name: "Dual Unique Mechanism Framework"
    description: "Every promotion must have two mechanisms: (1) Unique mechanism behind the PROBLEM - explains why the prospect failed in the past, and (2) Unique mechanism behind the SOLUTION - explains why this product will succeed. Both are required."
    domain: "copywriting-mechanism"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-024"
    name: "Brief Questionnaire (11 Parts)"
    description: "The Brief has 11 parts: (1) Who is audience, (2) Pain points short/long term, (3) One big promise of the COPY, (4) Existing solutions and why they fail, (5) New claim/mechanism, (6) Paradoxical question, (7) Metaphor/analogy, (8) Bold claim about PRODUCT results, (9) What is the product, (10) Background story + discovery, (11) Headline ideas + upsell ideas."
    domain: "copywriting-brief"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-025"
    name: "7-Part Sales Letter Outline"
    description: "A complete sales letter has 7 macro components: (1) Lead/Introduction, (2) Background Story, (3) Unique Mechanism of Problem, (4) Unique Mechanism of Solution, (5) Product Buildup and Reveal, (6) Close, (7) FAQs."
    domain: "copywriting-structure"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-026"
    name: "Lead Structure (10 Elements)"
    description: "The lead must include: (1) Call out problem/pain point, (2) Promise solution + time/money savings, (3) Tease discovery story, (4) Tease unique mechanism, (5) Tease contrarian nature, (6) Fascinations for continued reading, (7) Address skepticism, (8) Credibility builders, (9) Qualifiers for who this is for, (10) Broad testimonials (not product-specific)."
    domain: "copywriting-leads"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-027"
    name: "Close Structure (12 Elements)"
    description: "The close contains: (1) Product details/USP, (2) Dismiss alternatives, (3) Build product value, (4) Additional testimonials, (5) How to use product, (6) Urgency, (7) Scarcity, (8) Personal mission tie-back, (9) Price justification vs alternatives, (10) Price reveal with savings, (11) Multi-package options, (12) Three calls to action with crossroads close and guarantee."
    domain: "copywriting-closing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-028"
    name: "7-Step Headline Checklist"
    description: "Seven elements of killer headlines: (1) Curiosity, (2) Calling out pain point, (3) Promising a solution, (4) Specificity, (5) Simplicity, (6) Credibility/addressing skepticism, (7) Timeframe to achieve results. Not all 7 required, but curiosity is always essential."
    domain: "copywriting-headlines"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-029"
    name: "11 Types of Fascination Bullets"
    description: "Eleven fascination bullet types: (1) Why bullet, (2) How bullet, (3) When bullet, (4) What bullet, (5) Secret bullet, (6) List bullet, (7) Never bullet, (8) Contrarian bullet, (9) Named Oddity bullet, (10) Speedy bullet, (11) Plus bullet (adds another dimension)."
    domain: "copywriting-fascinations"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-030"
    name: "Subject Line Matrix (15 Column Categories)"
    description: "A matrix framework for generating email subject lines with 15 angle categories: A-Promise, B-Problem/Dimensionalized, C-New Cause, D-New Mechanism, E-Known Remedy Wrong, F-Taboo as Solution, G-Dominant Emotion, H-Story, I-Conflict/Conspiracy, J-Problems of Normal Solution, K-People, L-Topical, M-Believability, N-Urgency/Scarcity, O-Targeted."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-031"
    name: "7-Step AOV Money Close"
    description: "Seven premises for increasing average order value: (1) Packages are limited, out-of-stocks are real, (2) Customers keep choosing larger package, (3) More you use it the better, (4) Experts recommend larger packages, (5) Life with each package size (life is best with biggest), (6) Small discount for smallest, largest discount for biggest, (7) CTA mentions the biggest package first."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-032"
    name: "Background Story Template"
    description: "Universal story structure: Spokesperson was in pain/suffering, tried traditional solutions that did not work, reached a breaking point trigger event, went on a quest, discovered unique mechanism of problem through trial and error, then discovered unique mechanism of solution, created/found product, got results, shared with others who also got results, now sharing with the world."
    domain: "copywriting-storytelling"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-033"
    name: "Big Idea Selection Criteria"
    description: "Five questions for choosing a big idea: (1) Can I quickly explain it to friends/family? (2) Can I do it without going back to the promo? (3) Is it instantly comprehended as important and exciting? (4) Does it lead to an inevitable conclusion that makes selling easy? (5) Does the contrarian aspect catch attention?"
    domain: "copywriting-big-idea"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "FW-SG-034"
    name: "Mechanism Discovery Process For Health"
    description: "Process for finding health mechanisms: (1) Go to Science Daily, (2) Search your topic broadly, (3) Open 10-15 articles in new tabs, (4) Copy-paste interesting findings into a research document, (5) Look for contrarian or new discoveries, (6) Evaluate which is easiest to explain, (7) Consider making up a memorable term for the mechanism, (8) Find supporting nutrients/ingredients."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

L5_METHODOLOGIES:
  - id: "MET-SG-015"
    name: "RMBC Full Execution Methodology"
    description: "Complete step-by-step: (1) R - Answer all research questions about demographics, existing solutions, curiosity, and corruption angles using Amazon reviews, forums, and Google. (2) M - Develop dual unique mechanism (problem + solution). (3) B - Fill out the 11-part Brief questionnaire writing in copy-ready prose. (4) C - Assemble the 7-part sales letter outline, plugging in Brief content and writing remaining sections."
    domain: "copywriting-methodology"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-016"
    name: "Research Execution Methodology"
    description: "Step-by-step research process: (1) Define demographic profile - age, religion, politics, economics. (2) List hopes, dreams, victories, failures. (3) Identify outside forces they blame. (4) Capture prejudices and core beliefs in 1-3 sentences. (5) List all existing solutions and what market likes/dislikes. (6) Find horror stories about existing solutions. (7) Search for old/historical solutions pre-1960. (8) Look for corruption/fall-from-Eden narratives. All answers sourced from Amazon reviews, forums, and Google."
    domain: "copywriting-research"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-017"
    name: "Brief-To-Copy Assembly Methodology"
    description: "Converting Brief to sales letter: (1) Copy-paste audience/pain points from Brief into Lead. (2) Reverse pain points to create promise. (3) Pull mechanism content directly from Brief. (4) Copy-paste background story from Brief. (5) Copy-paste product description from Brief. (6) Write product buildup/reveal (only 500-1000 words of original writing). (7) Assemble close using formulaic template. (8) Add FAQs from Amazon/competitor research. Result: 60-80% of letter comes directly from Brief."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-018"
    name: "Fascination Writing Methodology"
    description: "Process for writing fascinations: (1) Read through source material (book, guide, course content). (2) Identify interesting facts, statistics, secrets, counterintuitive findings. (3) Transform each into one of the 11 bullet types (why, how, when, what, secret, list, never, contrarian, named oddity, speedy, plus). (4) Add 'hint' extensions for extra curiosity. (5) Add page numbers for info products. (6) Use in lead, product descriptions, headlines, and ads."
    domain: "copywriting-fascinations"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-019"
    name: "AOV Money Close Execution"
    description: "Step-by-step close for multi-package offers: (1) Explain why packages are limited with real production reasons. (2) Mention how customers keep choosing larger packages. (3) Explain why more product usage is better with timeline details. (4) Have expert/doctor recommend the larger package. (5) Paint life with each package: single=good, 3-month=better, 6-month=best. (6) Show escalating discounts - small for 1, bigger for 3, biggest for 6. (7) CTA specifically mentions the largest package first."
    domain: "sales-conversion"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-020"
    name: "Headline Testing Methodology"
    description: "Process for writing and evaluating headlines: (1) Write multiple headline variations. (2) Score each against the 7-step checklist (curiosity, pain point, solution, specificity, simplicity, credibility, timeframe). (3) Build headline complexes with pre-header, main headline, and post-header. (4) Use fascination bullets in the post-header for additional hooks. (5) Test lead-in approach order (can start with pain, curiosity, or contrarian angle)."
    domain: "copywriting-headlines"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-021"
    name: "Big Idea Research And Selection Process"
    description: "Methodology for finding the big idea: (1) Complete your research on the market and their pain points. (2) Identify the traditional explanation of problem and solution. (3) Search for new/contrarian explanations via Science Daily and news. (4) Evaluate each against the meme test - can you explain it in one sentence? (5) Check if it leads inevitably to wanting the product. (6) Pick the one that is most intuitively understandable and shareable. (7) Verify you can find evidence to support the contrarian angle."
    domain: "copywriting-big-idea"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"

  - id: "MET-SG-022"
    name: "Subject Line Matrix Population Method"
    description: "Process for generating subject lines: (1) Set up a 15-column by 29-row matrix spreadsheet. (2) Use columns A-O for the 15 angle categories. (3) For each row, write one subject line per angle category, generating up to 29 variations per angle. (4) Mix and match angles for hybrid subject lines. (5) Test different columns for different email sends to the same list."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-METHOD"
```
