```yaml
  - id: "FIL-SG-091"
    name: "Experimenter and Scientist Mentality"
    description: "Approach copywriting and funnel building as an experimenter and scientist. Most experiments yield incremental gains, not breakthroughs. Focus on doing the work rather than endlessly tweaking processes."
    domain: "copywriting-mindset"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FIL-SG-092"
    name: "Swipe Structure Not Words"
    description: "When modeling successful copy, extract the structural outline and template rather than copying words. Feed AI the structure plus a new brief to create something that models the same framework but is original."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FIL-SG-093"
    name: "Funnel Economics Drive Everything"
    description: "You cannot do marketing without understanding funnel economics. AOV, CAC, COGS, LTV, and margin determine whether a business scales or dies. Upsells and email sequences are not optional â€” they're economic necessities."
    domain: "funnel-economics"
    source: "RMBC-II"

L2_MENTAL_MODELS:
  - id: "MM-SG-060"
    name: "Two Customer Journeys"
    description: "Journey 1: Prospect to Purchaser (point A to B). Journey 2: Customer to Repeat Purchaser (B to C, D, E...). Different copy and strategies apply to each journey."
    domain: "customer-journey"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-061"
    name: "Five Product-Market Awareness Levels"
    description: "Based on Eugene Schwartz: Unaware, Problem Aware, Solution Aware, Product Aware, Most Aware. Each level requires dramatically different copy approaches, mechanisms, and funnel structures."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-062"
    name: "Awareness Level Determines Funnel Fat"
    description: "High awareness = thin funnel (ad â†’ product page â†’ cart). Low awareness = fat funnel (ad â†’ advertorial â†’ VSL â†’ product page â†’ checkout â†’ nurture emails). Rate awareness on a scale to determine funnel structure."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-063"
    name: "Platform Congruence Model"
    description: "YouTube users are in video-watching mode (video ads â†’ video landing pages). Native ad users are in article-reading mode (text ads â†’ advertorials). Meta users are in scrolling mode (short engaging content â†’ quick landing pages)."
    domain: "platform-strategy"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-064"
    name: "UMP-UMS Two-Part Logical Connection"
    description: "Unique Mechanism of the Problem (UMP) explains the real reason past solutions failed. Unique Mechanism of the Solution (UMS) explains why this product will succeed. Together they form a logical problem-solution bridge."
    domain: "unique-mechanisms"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-065"
    name: "Missing 1% Knowledge Gap"
    description: "The unique mechanism works by showing prospects that their knowledge about their problem is incomplete. They've been missing one crucial piece of information â€” the knowledge gap that's been holding them back."
    domain: "unique-mechanisms"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-066"
    name: "Side Hustle Sandbox Trap"
    description: "People repeatedly fail at side hustles because they operate in a sandbox of low-leverage activities (dropshipping, freelancing, gig work) where they're disposable cogs. The real distinction is between activity (trading time for money) and assets (cash-flowing systems)."
    domain: "bizop-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-067"
    name: "Activity vs Assets Dichotomy"
    description: "Past failures stem from pursuing income-gap solutions (more work = more money) instead of asset-gap solutions (controlling cash-flowing assets). Reframing from hustler to operator/investor is the paradigm shift."
    domain: "bizop-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-068"
    name: "Urgency Decay Over Time"
    description: "As time passes from the moment of peak pain/desire, urgency and desire decrease. The further removed from the pain point, the less likely someone buys. Copy must reset urgency or capture it at peak."
    domain: "buying-psychology"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-069"
    name: "AOV as Scaling Lever"
    description: "The difference between a failed business and an 8-figure business often comes down to AOV. Moving from negative margin to positive margin through upsells unlocks the ability to reinvest in customer acquisition and scale."
    domain: "funnel-economics"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-070"
    name: "Buyer Mode Window"
    description: "The number one time to get someone to buy again is moments after they just bought. They're already in buyer mode, credit card is out, mental commitment is made. This is the optimal window for upsells."
    domain: "upsell-strategy"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-071"
    name: "Demonstrable Proof Shortcut"
    description: "Products with immediate visual demonstrable proof (before/after, instant results) need significantly less copy and shorter funnels. Good animations and visuals can substitute for demonstrable proof in products that lack instant visible results."
    domain: "conversion-optimization"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-072"
    name: "Paradise Lost Narrative"
    description: "A powerful messaging framework where things used to be good (Garden of Eden), then outside forces corrupted everything (Big Pharma, Big Food, government), and now we're bringing back the forgotten solution."
    domain: "storytelling"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MM-SG-073"
    name: "Entrepreneurial Trauma Loop"
    description: "Repeated failures create entrepreneurial trauma causing hyper-vigilance and cynicism. Paradoxically, fear of loss drives people back to low-risk low-reward options (the sandbox), perpetuating failure. Marketing must break this psychological feedback loop."
    domain: "bizop-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

L3_HEURISTICS:
  - id: "HEUR-SG-090"
    name: "2-4 VSLs Per Day with AI"
    description: "Using the RMBC II process with Claude projects, a skilled copywriter can produce 2-4 high-quality VSLs in a single day, compared to weeks in the past."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-091"
    name: "20-40 Good Ads Per Day"
    description: "With the right AI process and brief, you can create 20-40 good ads in a single day."
    domain: "ad-creation"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-092"
    name: "Complete Upsell in 15 Minutes"
    description: "Once you have the AI process set up and the brief, a complete upsell page can be generated in about 15 minutes."
    domain: "upsell-strategy"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-093"
    name: "Email Sequences in 10 Minutes"
    description: "With Claude projects and a solid brief, email sequences (abandoned cart, welcome, promotional, winback) can be generated in roughly 10 minutes of active work."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-094"
    name: "Ingredient Research in Under 5 Minutes"
    description: "Using Perplexity's deep research with the ingredient research prompt, you can get complete studies, claims, and citations for any supplement formulation in less than 5 minutes."
    domain: "supplement-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-095"
    name: "Problem Aware Is Broadest Market"
    description: "For most products, especially bizop, the problem-aware segment is the largest addressable market. Mechanisms aimed at product-aware or solution-aware people reach only 10-25% of the total market."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-096"
    name: "15% Abandoned Cart Recovery Target"
    description: "A reasonable target is recovering 15% of checkout abandoners through email, SMS, and call center. This can dramatically change funnel economics from unprofitable to scalable."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-097"
    name: "Short Advertorial + Long PDP or Long Advertorial + Short PDP"
    description: "The selling has to happen somewhere. If the advertorial is short, the product page needs to be long (mini sales letter). If the advertorial is long and does heavy selling, the product page can be minimal (just product selection and checkout)."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-098"
    name: "Mobile-First PDP Design"
    description: "Pretty much all DTC traffic is mobile. Product pages should be designed and evaluated mobile-first. What looks weird on desktop often looks perfect on mobile."
    domain: "conversion-optimization"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-099"
    name: "Mechanism Complexity Inversely Correlates with Awareness"
    description: "Problem-aware markets need simpler mechanisms. Solution/product-aware markets need more sophisticated differentiation. Don't over-complicate mechanisms for broad audiences."
    domain: "unique-mechanisms"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-100"
    name: "Gemini Deep Research Best for Mechanisms"
    description: "For unique mechanism ideation, Gemini (especially deep research mode) produces significantly better and more nuanced output than ChatGPT or Claude deep research. ChatGPT tends to produce superficial 'cute nicknames' without substance."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-101"
    name: "8 Swipe Transcripts Per Claude Project"
    description: "For Claude VSL projects, approximately 8 example transcripts of the target length provides enough structural variety for the model to synthesize without overwhelming it."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-102"
    name: "ADHD Hook Format for 3-7 Minute VSLs"
    description: "3-7 minute video ads (ADHD hook format) are a highly effective format that runs as in-feed ads on Meta. They typically don't reveal the product, instead teasing a breakthrough with a CTA to watch a video or read a report."
    domain: "video-ads"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-103"
    name: "Subscription Justifies Negative Day-Zero"
    description: "For subscription businesses, going negative on day-zero customer acquisition is a totally fine model because LTV from rebills recovers the investment. But only if retention is strong enough."
    domain: "funnel-economics"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-104"
    name: "Six Reasons People Abandon Cart"
    description: "1) Don't fully believe solution works 2) Negative past experiences 3) Lack of self-confidence/need outside approval 4) Price perceived as too high 5) Life gets in the way 6) Lack of urgency in the offer."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-105"
    name: "Trend Hijacking Needs Minimal Copy"
    description: "When a product rides a massive trend where everyone already believes it works (like GLP-1 medications), you need minimal copy â€” just trust signals, pricing, and social proof. No VSL or heavy selling needed."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-106"
    name: "Brand Equity Reduces Funnel Length"
    description: "The more recognized and favorably perceived a brand is, the less copy is required. Over time, as brand equity builds, you may find VSLs become unnecessary and simple UGC/social proof + product pages are sufficient."
    domain: "brand-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-107"
    name: "Immediate Pain Point Over Aspirational"
    description: "A product solving an immediate acute pain point (migraine today) needs far less copy than one selling aspirational outcomes (anti-aging someday). For long-term benefits, find a symptomatic immediate pain point to lead with."
    domain: "copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "HEUR-SG-108"
    name: "Psychographic Questions Over Demographic"
    description: "Demographic research (age, income) tells you facts. Psychographic research (hopes, fears, beliefs, prejudices, attitudes) tells you how to speak to people. Psychographics drive better copy."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

L4_FRAMEWORKS:
  - id: "FW-SG-060"
    name: "RMBC II Process"
    description: "Research (product-market awareness + competitor + psychographic + unified) â†’ Mechanism (ideation + evaluation + expansion) â†’ Brief 2.0 (25-point AI-optimized document) â†’ Copy (Claude Projects for any asset type)."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-061"
    name: "Four-Step Deep Research Process"
    description: "Step 1: Product-Market Awareness research (5 levels). Step 2: Competitor Research (claims, likes, dislikes). Step 3: Psychographic Research (hopes, fears, beliefs, prejudices). Step 4: Unified Research Document (combine all three into one brief for LLMs)."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-062"
    name: "Brief 2.0 (25-Point AI Brief)"
    description: "Expanded brief optimized for LLM consumption with 25 fields: audience, pain points, future pacing (physical/emotional/social/psychological), existing solutions, mechanism detail, mechanism nickname, ingredients/studies, bold claims, product features, story, speakers, credibility builders, pricing, options, guarantee, bonuses, scarcity, objections, and hook ideas."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-063"
    name: "UMP Ideation-Evaluation-Expansion Process"
    description: "Step 1: Generate many mechanism ideas using LLMs with unified research + awareness level targeting. Step 2: Evaluate mechanisms for market fit using a scoring prompt. Step 3: Expand the winning mechanism into copy-ready format. Step 4: Create a Claude Project for mechanism development."
    domain: "unique-mechanisms"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-064"
    name: "Claude VSL Projects Architecture"
    description: "Create separate Claude Projects for each VSL length: long-form (20-50 min), medium (6-18 min), short (3-7 min), micro (1-2 min). Each project contains ~8 swipe transcripts + generation instructions. Feed the brief â†’ get synthesized original copy."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-065"
    name: "15 Factors Funnel Structure Scoring"
    description: "Rate 15 factors (1-5) to determine optimal funnel structure: market awareness, demonstrable proof, price point, traffic temperature, brand equity, perceived risk, regulatory compliance, offer complexity, LTV importance, urgency of problem, competitor saturation, buying context/device, payment friction, social proof assets, time to results. High total score = lean funnel, low score = heavy funnel."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-066"
    name: "PDP Template Extraction Process"
    description: "Step 1: Find successful product pages. Step 2: Feed to Claude with prompt to create templatized structural outline. Step 3: Give the outline + brief for a new product to Claude. Step 4: Generate a new PDP following the winning structure with new content."
    domain: "product-pages"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-067"
    name: "Psychographic Research Question Framework"
    description: "10 categories of psychographic questions: Who is your customer (demo), Attitude/beliefs, Social/economic views, Political leanings, Hopes and dreams, Victories and failures, Outside forces preventing success, Prejudices/biases, Core beliefs about life, Existing solutions tried."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-068"
    name: "Curiosity and Corruption Angles"
    description: "Two powerful copy angles from psychographic research: Curiosity (has someone tried to solve this in a unique way? forgotten solutions? pre-1960 remedies?) and Corruption (did the problem used to not exist? what outside forces caused it? Big Pharma, Big Food, Big Government narratives)."
    domain: "copywriting-angles"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-069"
    name: "Common Funnel Structures 2025"
    description: "Structure 1: Ad â†’ PDP â†’ Cart (high awareness). Structure 2: Ad â†’ Advertorial â†’ PDP â†’ Cart (most common for 8-figure brands). Structure 3: Ad â†’ Advertorial â†’ VSL â†’ Checkout (DR heavy). Structure 4: Ad â†’ VSL â†’ Checkout (classic DR). Structure 5: In-feed video ad â†’ PDP â†’ Cart (modern ecom)."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-070"
    name: "Abandoned Cart Sequence Framework"
    description: "Address the 6 reasons people abandon: Email 1 (life interrupted - urgency reset). Email 2 (incomplete belief - social proof/education). Email 3 (price objection - value reframe/discount). Email 4 (confidence/approval - testimonials from similar people). Email 5 (final urgency - last chance)."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-071"
    name: "Upsell Strategy Categories"
    description: "Five categories of what to sell as upsells: 1) Better results (premium version), 2) Faster results (accelerator), 3) Improved version (upgrade), 4) New problems created by purchase (complementary), 5) Expanded problems (comprehensive solution)."
    domain: "upsell-strategy"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-072"
    name: "Email Sequence Types"
    description: "Four core email sequence types: 1) Abandoned Cart (recover non-buyers), 2) Welcome/Consumption (onboard + cross-sell), 3) Promotional (holiday sales, flash sales), 4) Winback/Reorder (reactivate lapsed customers, trigger reorders)."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "FW-SG-073"
    name: "Ingredient Research Prompt Structure"
    description: "Feed AI: product ingredients + unified research + mechanism. Ask for claims tied to NCBI/scientific journal studies. Prioritize RCTs with humans. Request claims focused on: 1) how ingredient supports mechanism, 2) additional pain point alleviation. Keep digestible with references."
    domain: "supplement-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

L5_METHODOLOGIES:
  - id: "MET-SG-045"
    name: "Unified Research Document Creation Method"
    description: "Step 1: Run product-market awareness deep research. Step 2: Run competitor research. Step 3: Run psychographic research. Step 4: Export all three as PDFs. Step 5: Upload to Claude with unification prompt requesting: target demo, psychographic overview, pain points, hopes/dreams, language to use/avoid, primary promises, objections, existing solutions. Output is one master research brief."
    domain: "market-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-046"
    name: "Brief 2.0 Generator Method"
    description: "Step 1: Create Claude Project with Brief 2.0 template as instructions. Step 2: Upload unified research document + mechanism document + ingredient research as knowledge. Step 3: Provide product URL or basic details. Step 4: Claude generates complete 25-point brief. Step 5: Human reviews and adjusts for accuracy."
    domain: "copywriting-process"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-047"
    name: "AI-Assisted VSL Production Method"
    description: "Step 1: Complete research and brief. Step 2: Open appropriate length Claude Project (long/medium/short). Step 3: Feed brief. Step 4: Request outline first. Step 5: Provide feedback on outline. Step 6: Request full VSL draft. Step 7: Copy-chief: review, question, revise. Step 8: Iterate until satisfied. Total time: 2-4 hours for long-form."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-048"
    name: "UMP Mechanism Selection Method"
    description: "Step 1: Generate 10+ mechanisms using multiple LLMs (Gemini deep research preferred). Step 2: Filter by product-market awareness alignment. Step 3: Copy promising mechanisms to evaluation document. Step 4: Feed evaluation document + unified research to Claude. Step 5: Claude ranks mechanisms by conversion potential. Step 6: Expand winner into copy-ready format."
    domain: "unique-mechanisms"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-049"
    name: "PDP Swipe-and-Create Method"
    description: "Step 1: Find 1-2 successful product pages in similar vertical. Step 2: Feed URLs to Claude asking for templatized structural outline. Step 3: Save outline as template. Step 4: Feed template + brief for new product to Claude. Step 5: Claude writes new PDP following winning structure with new content. Step 6: Copy-chief and refine."
    domain: "product-pages"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-050"
    name: "Advertorial-to-Claude Project Method"
    description: "Step 1: Collect successful advertorial examples (short and long). Step 2: Create Claude Project with advertorial instructions + example transcripts. Step 3: Feed brief + target platform context. Step 4: Specify length and whether advertorial goes to VSL, PDP, or checkout. Step 5: Generate and iterate."
    domain: "advertorial-creation"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-051"
    name: "15-Factor Funnel Diagnostic Method"
    description: "Step 1: Rate each of 15 factors on 1-5 scale (5 = needs least copy). Step 2: Sum total score. Step 3: Score 46+ = lean funnel (ad â†’ PDP â†’ cart). Score 30-45 = medium funnel (ad â†’ quiz/advertorial â†’ VSL/PDP â†’ cart). Score below 30 = heavy funnel (ad â†’ advertorial â†’ long VSL â†’ checkout â†’ nurture emails). Step 4: Select structure and begin copy creation."
    domain: "funnel-architecture"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-052"
    name: "Multi-LLM Deep Research Method"
    description: "Step 1: Run same research prompt in 3+ LLMs (Perplexity, Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude). Step 2: Compare outputs â€” each LLM has different strengths. Step 3: Perplexity best for ingredient research with citations. Gemini deep research best for mechanism ideation. Step 4: Combine best outputs into unified document."
    domain: "ai-research"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-053"
    name: "Claude Project Email Sequence Generator Method"
    description: "Step 1: Create Claude Project with email sequence type instructions (abandoned cart, welcome, promotional, winback). Step 2: Upload brief + product details. Step 3: Specify sequence type + number of emails + product type (supplement, SaaS, coaching, etc.). Step 4: Generate complete sequence. Step 5: Customize for brand voice."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-054"
    name: "Hook Generation at Scale Method"
    description: "Step 1: Feed brief + winning hook examples to Claude. Step 2: Request hundreds of hook variations across different angles (fear, curiosity, benefit, story, statistic, question). Step 3: Filter for best based on product-market awareness level. Step 4: Test at scale on Meta/TikTok."
    domain: "ad-creation"
    source: "RMBC-II"

  - id: "MET-SG-055"
    name: "Copy-Chiefing AI Output Method"
    description: "Step 1: Review AI-generated copy as a copy chief, not a writer. Step 2: Question every section â€” 'Does this make sense? Is this the strongest angle? What's missing?' Step 3: Tell Claude what to change with strategic direction. Step 4: Iterate 2-4 rounds until the copy reads as if an expert wrote it. Step 5: Final human polish on voice and brand consistency."
    domain: "ai-copywriting"
    source: "RMBC-II"
```

- **L1 PHILOSOPHIES:** 14 elements (FIL-SG-080 to FIL-SG-093)

**Total: 72 new elements from RMBC II**

Key domains covered:
- **AI-Assisted Copywriting** (the core innovation of RMBC II â€” using Claude Projects as copy engines)
- **Product-Market Awareness** (the foundational strategic concept threading through everything)
- **Funnel Architecture** (the 15-factor scoring system, common 2025 structures)
- **Unique Mechanism Development** (AI-powered ideation, evaluation, expansion)
- **Brief 2.0** (the 25-point LLM-optimized creative brief)
- **Platform-Specific Strategy** (Meta, Native, YouTube, TikTok, Google)
- **Funnel Economics** (AOV, CAC, LTV, margin calculations driving all decisions)
- **Email Marketing Systems** (abandoned cart, welcome, promotional, winback)
- **PDP/Advertorial/Checkout/Upsell** optimization</result>

    name: "Everything Is Testable"

    description: "Template: Tell the customer's backstory (struggle) â†’ Share their journey/transformation â†’ Connect it to the product/service â†’ Simple CTA to learn more or buy. Format: You/brand first, their story, back to CTA."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "FW-SG-125"
    name: "FAQ Email Conversion Framework"
    description: "Send FAQ emails to fence-sitters: Subject line about questions flooding in â†’ Brief logistic FAQ first â†’ Detailed content FAQ (from sales letter) â†’ CTA after each FAQ section â†’ Sign off. Effective when prospects are interested but unconverted."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "FW-SG-126"
    name: "Ad Creative Selection Criteria"
    description: "5 criteria for selecting winning ad examples to model: (1) Running 2+ months consistently, (2) High social proof/engagement visible, (3) From reputable brand/page, (4) Follows winning framework from templates, (5) Hits at least 3 key checklist points."
    domain: "facebook-ads"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

L5_METHODOLOGIES:
  - id: "MET-SG-080"
    name: "Profit Fix Formula Funnel Audit Process"
    description: "Step-by-step: (1) Map all 5 funnel levers (adsâ†’LPâ†’checkoutâ†’upsellâ†’email), (2) Apply corresponding checklist to each lever, (3) Identify missing/weak elements, (4) Prioritize tests by potential impact, (5) Run A/B tests on highest-impact elements first, (6) Measure, iterate, repeat."
    domain: "funnel-optimization"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-081"
    name: "Landing Page Breakdown Analysis"
    description: "Process for analyzing any landing page: (1) Open checklist side-by-side, (2) Score each checklist element as present/absent, (3) For present elements, assess quality, (4) For absent elements, assess potential impact of adding, (5) Note what to test, (6) Compare to templates for structural gaps."
    domain: "landing-page-design"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-082"
    name: "Facebook Ad Testing Methodology"
    description: "Process: (1) Write best first draft of ad or have expert write it, (2) Find control audiences first based on winning ad, (3) Once control audience found, test new ads to that audience, (4) Limit testing spend to 10% of budget or $100/day, (5) Establish control creative ASAP, (6) Keep flow of new creatives testing at budget cap."
    domain: "facebook-ads"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-083"
    name: "Upsell Video Production Process"
    description: "Steps: (1) Open with congratulations (3-5 minutes of pure reinforcement), (2) Switch to B-roll when transitioning to offer (re-engage visually), (3) Present new problem, (4) Build consequences, (5) Reveal solution with DFY/more/faster angle, (6) Price anchor from high to low, (7) Show CTA button only near end of video, (8) Include decline link and exit pop down-sell."
    domain: "upsell-optimization"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-084"
    name: "Email Urgency Campaign Sequence"
    description: "Process: (1) Establish the sale/offer with initial email, (2) Send urgency email tying to emotional story or relatable scenario, (3) Use conversational tone with personality, (4) Include clear time-limited deadline ('3.5 hours left'), (5) End with philosophical question ('What's going to change if you don't act now?'), (6) Follow with last chance email."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-085"
    name: "Swipe File Building From Ads Library"
    description: "Process: (1) Go to Facebook Ads Library, (2) Search competitor/brand pages, (3) Filter by active ads, (4) Identify ads running 2+ months, (5) Note hook, structure, creative type, (6) Screenshot and catalog by niche/type, (7) Study patterns across multiple brands in your vertical."
    domain: "facebook-ads"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-086"
    name: "Template Application Methodology"
    description: "Process for using PFF templates: (1) Select template matching your funnel type, (2) Treat bracketed sections as prompts not limits, (3) Expand copy blocks beyond template box sizes, (4) Install heat map tracking before launch, (5) Run A/B test of template version vs current page, (6) Optimize based on heat map data showing where people click and drop off."
    domain: "conversion-optimization"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-087"
    name: "Checkout Page Optimization Process"
    description: "Steps: (1) Ensure branding/logo/contact visible, (2) Place order form on right side, (3) Add social proof near form, (4) Add urgency elements (timer, stock), (5) Show offer stack image, (6) Include trust seals multiple times, (7) Add guarantee badge, (8) Test order bump but monitor upsell impact on AOV, (9) Add exit pop with coupon/discount."
    domain: "checkout-optimization"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-088"
    name: "Multi-Package Pricing Display Method"
    description: "Steps: (1) Offer 3 tiers (1/3/5 or 1/3/6 units), (2) Place best value in center position, (3) Show price PER UNIT not total price, (4) Label middle as 'Most Popular' or 'Best Value', (5) Show savings amount/percentage, (6) Use subscribe-and-save only for single unit option, (7) Include security icons under each CTA button."
    domain: "pricing-psychology"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-089"
    name: "Pattern Interrupt Ad Creative Method"
    description: "Steps for creating pattern interrupt ads: (1) Choose unexpected visual metaphor (digging with pen, Grinch costume), (2) Use diagonal lines to break feed's parallel patterns, (3) Boost image contrast ~20%, (4) Include human face/eyes for eye-attraction, (5) Make it look native to platform (could be a friend's post), (6) Add depth/layers to image composition."
    domain: "facebook-ads"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

  - id: "MET-SG-090"
    name: "Discount Email Launch Sequence"
    description: "Steps: (1) Write compelling subject line with exact discount ('Save 90% on RMBC Method'), (2) Open with direct announcement of sale, (3) Explain what the product is for new readers, (4) Stack benefits and social proof, (5) Reveal discounted price vs normal price, (6) Include clear link and time limit, (7) Follow up with urgency and last chance emails over 3-day window."
    domain: "email-marketing"
    source: "PROFIT-FIX-FORMULA"

## L2 MENTAL MODELS (MM-BS-2XX)

MM-BS-200: "A Pile / B Pile Email Model" (Gary Halbert adapted) -- people sort email into personal (must open) vs commercial (delete). Make emails look A-pile: plain text, real name From line, personal salutation, misspellings, conversational tone | domain: email-deliverability | source: Issue_19

MM-BS-201: "The Ironman Offer Model" (Ken McCarthy) -- there are people on your list not content with average who will buy insanely expensive, ballsy offers. Create offers so outrageous they take YOUR breath away. The rest buy the t-shirt and popcorn | domain: pricing-strategy | source: Issue_20

MM-BS-202: "Winners vs Losers Contrast Model" -- create emails that list winner thinking vs loser thinking about your topic, challenge readers to assess themselves. Combines repetition, challenge psychology, and "take this test" headline formula | domain: email-format | source: Issue_19

MM-BS-203: "Market Research Avatar Model" -- 45+ deep questions about your prospect (fears, pains, humiliations, anger, enemies, income, politics, slang, fantasies, fun, hobbies, complaints, revenge desires) to know them better than they know themselves | domain: market-research | source: Issue_21

MM-BS-204: "Sequential Selling Model" (Sean D'Souza) -- consumers are conditioned to buy in sequence (Star Wars trilogy, karate belt colors, restaurant appetizer-to-dessert). Force a specific buying sequence: opt-in â†’ flagship product â†’ continuity â†’ back-end | domain: funnel-design | source: Issue_21

MM-BS-205: "Email Copywriter vs Email Marketer Model" -- two camps: copywriters obsess over subject lines/metrics/quality, marketers obsess over speed/sales/frequency. Goal is to be "bipolar email badass" embodying best of both | domain: email-strategy | source: Issue_33

MM-BS-206: "The Philosophy Email Model" -- emails that pontificate on your worldview about the problem your product solves, getting in the reader's world without hard teaching or moving free lines. Sells by aligning thinking, not pitching | domain: email-format | source: Issue_33

MM-BS-207: "Masochistic Customer Model" -- your market LOVES reading about their symptoms and pain. It helps them bond with you, proves you understand them, and gives them their daily "drama fix" | domain: buyer-psychology | source: Issue_17

MM-BS-208: "The Clockwork Orange Language Model" -- create your own unique lexicon of invented words (like "goo-roo", "gluteus assimus", "the green stuff") that form a personal language. Readers learn it, competitors who copy look like wannabes | domain: branding | source: Issue_20

MM-BS-209: "One-A-Day Marketing Vitamin" (Gary Bencivenga) -- read one control ad or email per day and try to think of ways to improve it. Trains your brain to find flaws in great work, making you better fast | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_23

MM-BS-210: "Hathaway Shirt Identity Model" (David Ogilvy) -- find one unique, eccentric, original visual identity element (like Ogilvy's eyepatch model) that sticks in people's psychology, sets you apart, and makes copycats look like fools | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue_24

MM-BS-211: "The Abundance Mindset Model" -- whether dating or selling, knowing there are millions of prospects removes neediness. Never put up with abusive customers/clients. Walk away without hesitation | domain: business-mindset | source: Issue_31

MM-BS-212: "Dear Abby Advice Column Email Model" -- write emails that sound like advice you're giving to someone with the same problem as your reader. People love advice columns. Doesn't look/feel like a pitch but IS a pitch | domain: email-format | source: Issue_18

MM-BS-213: "The Eulogy Email Model" -- when a guru or influential figure in your market dies, write about what you learned from them. Their prestige rubs off on you, creating credibility and positioning | domain: email-format | source: Issue_22

MM-BS-214: "Comparison Testimonial Model" -- when someone compares you to a popular character (Seinfeld of email, Heisenberg of email), MILK IT in subject lines, emails, and testimonial pages as "sound byte" testimonials | domain: social-proof | source: Issue_34

MM-BS-215: "Shackleton Candor Model" -- selectively admit big flaws/drawbacks to make your 1-2 benefits extremely credible. Shackleton's ad had 6 negatives and 2 benefits, yet got overwhelming response | domain: credibility | source: Issue_34

## L3 HEURISTICS (HEUR-BS-2XX)

HEUR-BS-200: Removing the first name field from opt-in forms (asking only for email address) bumps opt-ins measurably | domain: list-building | source: Issue_17

HEUR-BS-201: A lightbox pop-up removal cost 300+ subscribers over a few months -- always keep pop-ups despite complaints | domain: list-building | source: Issue_17

HEUR-BS-202: Your opt-in bribe should have REAL retail value -- give away something that actually sells or has sold for real money | domain: list-building | source: Issue_17

HEUR-BS-203: When split testing, only test BIG variables (totally different pop-ups in word usage, colors, images) -- small changes rarely show meaningful differences | domain: testing | source: Issue_17

HEUR-BS-204: Mail 1 email/day to main list during a special sale, but 2/day to sublist (people specifically interested) -- get more aggressive with the sublist | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-205: On the final day of a sale, send 4 emails to main list and 5-7 emails to sublist creating an "eBay-like" countdown effect (Senoff countdown sequence) | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-206: A 30.8% clickthrough-to-sales conversion is achievable with a well-structured special sale using proper sequencing | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-207: First 5-6 days after a subscriber joins, do blatant pitch emails to drive away freebie seekers or convert serious buyers | domain: list-management | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-208: List (traffic) = 50% of results. Offer = 30%. Copy = 20%. Fix the traffic/list quality FIRST | domain: direct-response | source: Issue_30

HEUR-BS-209: Tell subscribers at 3 points it's daily email: (1) at opt-in, (2) in welcome email, (3) periodically in daily emails -- eliminates complaints about frequency | domain: list-management | source: Issue_30

HEUR-BS-210: Use ONE link in emails, usually at the end above signature -- less thought into placement means more natural/conversational flow | domain: email-format | source: Issue_33

HEUR-BS-211: Write emails in 15 minutes or less -- speed means more output, more projects, more income streams | domain: email-productivity | source: Issue_33

HEUR-BS-212: Read your best info products a minimum of 10 times -- you won't get all the gold until the 6th, 7th, even 10th reading | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_32

HEUR-BS-213: Write at LEAST 20 articles/emails per day for 30 days to force-develop email mastery | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_32

HEUR-BS-214: Hand-copy one ad per day from old-school direct mail copywriters (Halbert, Bencivenga) for 15 minutes -- creates neurological imprint of great copy | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_32

HEUR-BS-215: When someone sends a long rambling complaint email, reply with only "TL;DR" -- silences them and saves your time/money | domain: customer-management | source: Issue_20

HEUR-BS-216: Put the word "sale" in the subject line -- Matt Furey says it's guaranteed to get opened by people who usually ignore your emails (but don't overuse) | domain: email-subject-lines | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-217: Emails with LOW open rates and/or LOW click rates often sell BEST -- proven by MindValley data showing open rates stable but sales vary independently | domain: email-metrics | source: Issue_34

HEUR-BS-218: When you hit a powerful "nerve" topic, write several more emails about it for an entire week -- milk that theme until sales dwindle, not clicks | domain: email-strategy | source: Issue_33

HEUR-BS-219: "Encore presentations" -- re-use emails from 6, 9, 12 months earlier with hardly anyone noticing, and they can nab even more sales the second time | domain: email-productivity | source: Issue_24

HEUR-BS-220: Sell during holiday weekends (Thanksgiving, Christmas, July 4th, Labor Day) -- contrary to guru advice, people are bored and checking phones constantly | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_30

HEUR-BS-221: Subject lines that are casual ("hey") outperform formal/clever ones -- confirmed by Obama campaign as the best performer over duration | domain: email-subject-lines | source: Issue_22

HEUR-BS-222: "A strength overextended is no longer a strength" (Matt Furey) -- don't overuse the same subject line trick even if it works once | domain: email-strategy | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-223: Build a SUBLIST of people specifically interested in the topic you're doing a special sale for -- mail both main list and sublist simultaneously with different emails | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

HEUR-BS-224: Pithy emails sell better -- cut wasted words, rewrite in active voice, use analogies/figures of speech to "say more in less words" -- "when in doubt, chop it out" | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue_30

HEUR-BS-225: Never put 2 competing links in the same email -- the Twitter/social link steals clicks from your cash register | domain: email-format | source: Issue_30

## L4 FRAMEWORKS (FW-BS-2XX)

FW-BS-200: "Special Sale Email Sequence Framework" -- 4 steps: (1) Pick right product (affiliate preferred), (2) Create irresistible offer (90%+ discount + unique bonus), (3) Build sublist of interested people, (4) Load Thursday-Sunday email sequence on both lists | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

FW-BS-201: "Parris Lampropoulos Proof Structure" -- Story â†’ Mechanism â†’ Studies/Hard Data. Start with a story about someone with the problem, reveal the unique mechanism that makes your product work, then back it up with facts/science | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue_20

FW-BS-202: "The 6-Figure Email Lifestyle Business Model" -- 3 foundations (Continuity income, Speed over perfectionism, Scalability) + Market research (45 questions) + Tactical setup (Squeeze page, Sales letter, Email follow-up) + Sequential selling implementation | domain: business-model | source: Issue_21

FW-BS-203: "5 Funnel Models" -- (1) Opt-in â†’ Product â†’ Continuity â†’ Back-end, (2) Opt-in â†’ Continuity â†’ Back-end (BenSettle.com model), (3) Opt-in â†’ Continuity only, (4) Direct sales â†’ Continuity â†’ Back-end (no opt-in), (5) Opt-in â†’ Back-end only (no continuity) | domain: funnel-design | source: Issue_21

FW-BS-204: "Senoff Simple Sales Letter Template" -- Headline (just the product title) + "Here's what you get..." + component descriptions + Order link. Can be done in 10 minutes | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_21

FW-BS-205: "Schwartz 13 Intensification Methods for Email" -- (1) Present claims, (2) Put claims in action, (3) Bring in the reader, (4) Show reader how to test claims, (5) Stretch benefits over time, (6) Bring in an audience, (7) Show experts approving, (8) Compare/contrast/prove superiority, (9) Picture the black side, (10) Show how easy, (11) Use metaphor/analogy/imagination, (12) Summarize, (13) Put the guarantee to work | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue_17

FW-BS-206: "Disconnection Notice Email Framework" -- Last email in auto-responder sequence tells them this is their final chance, you won't hear from me again, includes a BIG discount as last-ditch irresistible offer to convert prospects to buyers | domain: email-automation | source: Issue_19

FW-BS-207: "10 Commandments of Email Mastery in 30 Days" -- (1) Write 20+ emails/articles per day, (2) Read Email Players Playbook 10x, (3) Hand-copy one great ad daily, (4) Listen to one talk radio episode daily, (5) Analyze one persuasive email daily, (6) Watch one Seinfeld episode daily, (7) Abstain from carnal activity, (8) Listen to standup comedy daily, (9) Write a personal email to someone daily, (10) Read your spam | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_32

FW-BS-208: "Talk Radio Email Format" -- Fill your head with market news/stories/conversations, then just write stream of consciousness tying anything into your product. Benefits: easy to write, direct response friendly, recyclable, sets you up as leader, downright fun | domain: email-format | source: Issue_24

FW-BS-209: "Anatomy of a Money-Making Email" (Breslin email analysis) -- 7 elements: (1) Tight/fascinating story, (2) Mailed during holiday, (3) Comes off genuine, (4) Pithy/no wasted words, (5) Just right amount of details, (6) Doesn't mislead, (7) No competing links | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue_30

FW-BS-210: "Online Dating Profile = Sales Letter Framework" -- Structure profile as series of mini-emails/stories: establish value without bragging â†’ show lifestyle benefits to reader â†’ describe work day (voyeur appeal) â†’ use stories/analogies â†’ describe ideal customer to repel 80% and attract 20% â†’ call to action | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_31

FW-BS-211: "Market Pimping Framework" -- Walk away from toxic clients, never be needy, have abundance mentality, maintain private sense of superiority, never apologize for selling, admit product flaws and spin into buying reasons, unsubscribe complainers, never sacrifice dignity for a sale | domain: customer-relations | source: Issue_34

## L5 METHODOLOGIES (MET-BS-2XX)

MET-BS-200: "Direct Mail Research to Email Method" -- (1) Get direct mail pieces in your market, (2) Highlight interesting facts/benefits/symptoms/stories with marker, (3) Each interesting item becomes the seed for an email, (4) Write about it in your own voice in ~200 words, (5) Segue into your product | domain: email-content | source: Issue_17

MET-BS-201: "Holiday Special Sale Execution Method" -- Thursday: story-driven email to main list + 2 emails to sublist announcing sale. Friday-Saturday: 1 email/day main list + 2/day sublist (content AM, reminder PM). Sunday: 4 emails to main list + 5-7 to sublist with countdown sequence (eBay-like urgency). Deadline: midnight | domain: email-sales | source: Issue_19

MET-BS-202: "Reply Survey Method" -- Send email offering $15 "in store credit" for answering ONE question: "What were the last 3 products about [topic] you bought?" Hit reply to answer. Then research those products' appeals, offers, funnels, and email strategies for competitive intel | domain: market-research | source: Issue_18

MET-BS-203: "High-Ticket Feeler Email Method" -- Write a transparent email describing a coaching/high-ticket offer you're considering. List what it would include, state the price ($3k+/month), and ask them to reply "I might be interested." If enough hands go up, create it. If not, you know not to | domain: product-validation | source: Issue_20

MET-BS-204: "Opt-In Transparency Method" -- At 3 touchpoints state emails are DAILY and promotional: (1) On squeeze page with explicit consent text ("I agree to receive DAILY email"), (2) In welcome email, (3) Periodically in daily emails. Result: virtually zero frequency complaints, happy Google/AdWords compliance | domain: list-management | source: Issue_30

MET-BS-205: "Email Mastery Training Method" (30-day intensive) -- Daily routine: Write 20+ pieces of copy + Hand-copy one ad 15 min + Listen to talk radio episode + Analyze one email for improvement + Watch Seinfeld episode + Listen to standup comedy + Write personal email to friend. No sexual activity for 30 days. Read spam daily | domain: skill-development | source: Issue_32

MET-BS-206: "Getting Clients via Email Method" -- Think like a client not a copywriter. Tell stories about clients you've helped. Every testimonial = multiple emails. Create checklists of what ads in the market do wrong. Talk from THEIR point of view (increasing sales, getting more bang for advertising buck, finding someone trustworthy). Demonstrate skills daily via emails | domain: client-acquisition | source: Issue_32

MET-BS-207: "Credibility Through Candor Email Method" -- Admit what you're NOT qualified to talk about, list things you suck at, admit product flaws openly. Then the things you DO claim become extremely believable. Entire email strategy built around honest self-deprecation that makes claims iron-clad (Shackleton principle) | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue_34

---

## L1 PHILOSOPHIES (FIL-BS-100 to FIL-BS-155)

FIL-BS-100: "If you want to get paid, get personal. The more personal and authentic your emails, the more you stand out from the hundreds of carbon-copy emails in their inbox." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

FIL-BS-101: "You are always safe in the other person's world. When talking about their pains, problems and desires, you cannot bore them, cannot tell them too much info, and your chances of making the sale go up dramatically." (adapted from Jim Camp) | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_02

FIL-BS-102: "The only real email rule I follow is to plug something every time, no matter what. Every email should contain a call to action, period." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

FIL-BS-103: "Send emails to your list every single day. Daily email is not optional -- it is the foundation of email marketing success." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_01

FIL-BS-104: "Subject lines should NOT be hypey or salesy. You want people to look forward to reading your emails and not deleting them on sight like they do with everyone else's." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_01

FIL-BS-105: "Rules can prevent you from doing things that will be profitable. I give you permission to break rules at your pleasure." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue_01

FIL-BS-106: "Teaching kills sales. Hard teaching in emails gives away what your product contains and removes the reason to buy. Soft teach -- tell them WHAT to do, not HOW." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_03

FIL-BS-107: "You will make far more money when you focus on their 'crab grass' instead of your 'weed killer.' Describe the problem in detail, but purposely tease and be vague about the solution." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_03

FIL-BS-108: "Curiosity always overpowers programming. Everyone is programmed to delete emails on sight. But if you embed curiosity into your subject lines, opening sentences, body copy and offers, you almost cannot lose." (from Stan Billue) | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_04

FIL-BS-109: "You're not building a 'list' -- you're building a fan base. Treat it accordingly." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_12

FIL-BS-110: "Don't just admit your product's negatives -- BRAG about them. Make the skeleton dance. Most people hide flaws; bragging about them builds massive trust." (from Barry Maher) | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_04

FIL-BS-111: "Sex is the ONE subject everyone wants to know more about. You can never bore people by talking about sex or dating." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

FIL-BS-112: "Slapping a high price on a quality product is one of the single BEST ways to create curiosity. You can't win on cutting price. But you can win on RAISING price." | domain: pricing-psychology | source: Issue_04

FIL-BS-113: "The whole point of email is to want people coming back tomorrow, and not deleting you on sight. Your daily emails must provide some kind of value to make it worth reading." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_01

FIL-BS-114: "Emails should feel like a conversation, not a sales pitch. If there's a secret to email, it's bonding with people just like a talk show host does. Think Oprah." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_05

FIL-BS-115: "A great product will do most of the heavy lifting for you. It's one of the big secrets of writing money-making emails." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_05

FIL-BS-116: "I am not a big fan of obsessing over clickthrus and open rates. You can get really high open rates and make less sales." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_07

FIL-BS-117: "Humor opens the mind to receive information. When you make people laugh, you bond with them in a way where they want to KEEP reading your emails forever." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

FIL-BS-118: "Repetition is GOOD in emails. It's kinda hypnotic. It keeps people focused on your message and is harder to pull away from." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_16

FIL-BS-119: "A purchase makes people buyers. But customers make a 'custom' of buying from you again and again. You don't build a real business on buyers, especially those who buy out of social obligation. You build it on customers." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue_09

FIL-BS-120: "Selling a product in email is like picking up women -- if you telegraph your desire too strongly, you fail. Be desire-less first, then be excellent, then be gone." | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_15

FIL-BS-121: "Writing emails that put yourself out on the chopping block ain't always easy. But 'Grow Some Balls' -- controversy, personal revelation, and shock effect get you big attention that translates into big sales." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_15

FIL-BS-122: "If it SHOULD make you nervous to push that send button, that's how you know you're on to something. You'll know you went too far when the market tells you." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

FIL-BS-123: "Writers write. You cannot get around this. The email 'workout' is writing each day." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

FIL-BS-124: "I read the 10 best marketing books 10 times. The loser says 'I have read over 100 marketing books 1 time.' Endless repetition ingrains concepts and feeds your subconscious." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue_16

FIL-BS-125: "Don't look for sympathy. Don't try to get people to feel sorry for you. Be emotional but not whiney. Be an authority -- a leader -- who gets the message across without sounding like a crybaby." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_16

FIL-BS-126: "The fantasy of what life will be like after using your product is what sells. It's your job to show them the fantasy they did not believe was possible -- and make it believable." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_05

FIL-BS-127: "Sell your own product. When you get good at email, you'll make FAR more selling your own product than affiliates. At the very least, license someone else's product so you get all the money." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue_02

FIL-BS-128: "In the land of emails, the dude who still uses the phone is king." (from John Francis Tighe, adapted) | domain: sales | source: Issue_03

FIL-BS-129: "There is NO better way to give yourself instant credibility, celebrity-appeal and expert status than getting in the media." | domain: marketing | source: Issue_03

FIL-BS-130: "The better you know your market, the more money you'll make. Market research is everything." | domain: marketing | source: Issue_02

---

## L2 MENTAL MODELS (MM-BS-100 to MM-BS-140)

MM-BS-100: "Crab Grass vs Weed Killer Model -- Focus on describing the problem (crab grass) in vivid detail. Be purposely vague about the solution (weed killer). They must buy your product for the solution." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_03

MM-BS-101: "The Curiosity Override Model -- Curiosity always overpowers programming. People are programmed to delete/ignore/hang up. Curiosity forces them to engage regardless of their resistance." | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_09

MM-BS-102: "The Talk Show Host Model -- The best email marketers are like Oprah: they get down from the expert soap box and become one with their audience. Bond first, sell second." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_05

MM-BS-103: "The $5 Milkshake Model -- High price creates curiosity and attention, not repulsion. People are fascinated by expensive things. Brag about high prices instead of apologizing." | domain: pricing-psychology | source: Issue_04

MM-BS-104: "The Dancing Skeleton Model -- Every product has negatives. Instead of hiding them, bring them out and brag about them. George Bernard Shaw: 'If you can't get rid of the family skeleton, you might as well make it dance.'" | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_04

MM-BS-105: "The Sacred Cow Defiling Model -- Find the heroes and beliefs your market holds dear, then skewer them. Show why those sacred cows are wrong. This overrides programming to delete because curiosity about 'what did he say about X?' is irresistible." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_09

MM-BS-106: "The Sublist Model -- A sublist is like an RSVP. People who opt in to hear about your launch have already taken a micro-commitment. They're pre-sold prospects, not cold leads." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

MM-BS-107: "The Implied Mechanism Model (from Mel Martin/Eugene Schwartz) -- Headlines and subject lines don't need big claims or benefits. An 'implied mechanism' creates an itch that MUST be scratched. 'What never to eat on an airplane' has no claim but is irresistible." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_11

MM-BS-108: "The Sequential Selling Model (from Sean D'Souza) -- People consume in sequences: Star Wars before Empire Strikes Back. White belt before green belt. Appetizer before entree. Sell products in a logical sequence, not randomly." | domain: business-strategy | source: Issue_13

MM-BS-109: "The 3rd Conversion Model (from Sean D'Souza) -- Once someone gets to the 3rd conversion (3rd purchase), they will buy everything you sell. The awful Star Wars prequels prove this: you have to REALLY like the originals to sit through them." | domain: business-strategy | source: Issue_13

MM-BS-110: "The Time Continuum Model -- Write capture pages using present-tense language that makes readers perceive time passing and events happening NOW. Replace 'will' and 'can' with '-ing' verbs and 'right now.' The reader is on a moving train and must hop on or miss out." | domain: conversion-optimization | source: Issue_08

MM-BS-111: "The Credibility Hijack Model -- Find testimonials from people with 'celebrity appeal' and build entire emails around them. You 'hijack' credibility from Apple Computer, Tony Robbins, etc. through their connection to your product." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

MM-BS-112: "The Loser vs Winner Model -- Frame contrasting beliefs as 'the loser says X, the winner says Y.' The repetitive structure is hypnotic and the reader naturally identifies with the 'winner' side." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_16

MM-BS-113: "The Forum Mining Model -- Forums and blog comments are gold mines. The most-viewed threads reveal what your market is actually thinking about. Use their EXACT language in your emails." | domain: market-research | source: Issue_11

MM-BS-114: "The Tao of Steve Model -- Three-part persuasion: (1) Be desire-less -- don't telegraph you want the sale, (2) Be excellent -- show value and skill, (3) Be gone -- take it away, create scarcity. 'We pursue that which retreats.'" | domain: sales-psychology | source: Issue_15

MM-BS-115: "The Matt Furey Method -- Rotate products randomly in daily emails. When you hit a 'nerve' (get lots of sales), keep running with that theme until sales die off. Then rotate to something else." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_13

MM-BS-116: "The Punch-Up Model (from punk rock) -- Like Social Distortion lyrics: simple on the surface but deep between the lines. Cautionary tales that are emotional but not whiney. Voice matches message." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_16

MM-BS-117: "The Application Email Model -- Instead of teaching what's in your product, teach them how they'll USE and APPLY your product after they buy. It FEELS like teaching but it's actually selling." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_12

MM-BS-118: "The Two-Emails-in-One Model -- Write your normal email with value/story, then in the PS, write a whole OTHER email that's pure pitch. If your email has enough value, readers will happily read the blatant pitch too." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_01

MM-BS-119: "The Contrast Effect Model -- High contrast gets BIG attention and is entertaining. 'Fat anorexic', 'one-legged man in an ass-kicking contest.' Use contrast in subject lines, stories, and analogies." | domain: copywriting | source: Issue_06

MM-BS-120: "The Specific Problem / Vague Solution Model -- Be very SPECIFIC about the problem and very VAGUE about the solution. This is arguably the single most important email writing skill. Master it and you'll never worry about sales again." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_10

---

## L3 HEURISTICS (HEUR-BS-100 to HEUR-BS-165)

HEUR-BS-100: "Plug something in EVERY email. The only real email rule: every email must contain a call to action to buy something." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-101: "Send emails DAILY. Not twice a week. Not when you feel like it. Every single day." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_01

HEUR-BS-102: "Insert your link in the body AND in the PS. Vary where you place your pitch -- body emails often outperform PS-only pitches because people skip the PS." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-103: "Write an email in 10-15 minutes. If it takes longer, you're overthinking it." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-104: "The 'ethically fibbing' discount technique should be used no more than once per list. More than that and it becomes an obvious gimmick." | domain: promotions | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-105: "Use curiosity subject lines more than any other type. Curiosity is one of the best kinds of subject lines you can use." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-106: "A good 25% of a list can come from JV relationships with other list owners. One endorsed mailing can mean 100+ new subscribers in 24 hours." | domain: list-building | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-107: "Pre-launch emails should run for about 2 months from first email to launch. That's a good time frame, but not always possible." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-108: "Pre-launch emails should be under 300 words. Just tease and get out. Tease and get out." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-109: "Launch process = 4 steps: (1) Announce, (2) Drive to sublist, (3) Tease both lists until launch, (4) eBay-style countdown on last day." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-110: "Magazine cover headlines are perfect subject line templates. Check Cosmo, Men's Health at Magazines.com for subject line fodder." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-111: "Longer solo ads produce more leads per click than shorter ones, even when shorter ads get more clicks. Don't optimize for clickthrus -- optimize for leads and sales." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_07

HEUR-BS-112: "Time Continuum landing pages increased opt-ins by 10-20%. Replace all future-tense verbs ('will', 'can') with present-tense '-ing' forms." | domain: conversion-optimization | source: Issue_08

HEUR-BS-113: "Time Continuum headlines work on squeeze pages but may NOT work on pop-ups. Pop-ups rely on interruption, not intent." | domain: conversion-optimization | source: Issue_08

HEUR-BS-114: "Write daily emails for 90 days minimum (90 'approaches'). At very least, do the 30-day challenge. This builds writing comfort, selling comfort, and income comfort." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

HEUR-BS-115: "Write 4-5 emails or MORE per day when starting out, not just 1. Then load into auto-responder and let them run while you reflect on results." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

HEUR-BS-116: "Go through your best marketing/copywriting resources 20+ times. Repetition ingrain concepts into your subconscious." | domain: business-education | source: Issue_14

HEUR-BS-117: "Watch one episode of Seinfeld a day to learn how to write 'an email about nothing' that entertains and sells." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

HEUR-BS-118: "Never hard teach in free emails. Tell them WHAT to do, but not HOW. They need your product for the HOW." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-119: "When doing humor emails that don't make direct sales, don't fret. The long-term bonding is worth more than any single sale." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

HEUR-BS-120: "Find 5-10 people you want to connect with. Use the JV email template. Some won't respond. But enough will to build meaningful relationships." | domain: list-building | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-121: "Include your PHONE NUMBER in JV outreach emails. 'Nobody else online does it.' It seals the deal." (David Dutton method) | domain: networking | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-122: "Use existing testimonials' credentials to sell. Google your customers. Find their celebrity appeal -- even a sheriff, brain surgeon, or local athlete counts." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

HEUR-BS-123: "Use IMDB.com forum thread titles as subject line inspiration. Comic book movie forums are especially goldmine for shock effect and curiosity subject lines." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_10

HEUR-BS-124: "Use Mel Martin 'fascination' bullet templates as email subject lines: 'What never to X', 'What X won't tell you', 'How to X your Y'. No claims needed -- just implied mechanisms." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_11

HEUR-BS-125: "Find your market's most-viewed forum posts. Those titles are already validated by attention -- use them (adapted) as email subject lines." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_11

HEUR-BS-126: "When launching, email both your main list AND sublist -- but with different emails at different frequencies." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-127: "Sean D'Souza's sequence: ONE front end product that brings them in. Don't sell anything else until they've bought the flagship." | domain: business-strategy | source: Issue_13

HEUR-BS-128: "If a particular email theme 'hits a nerve' and gets lots of sales, keep running with that theme until sales die off. Then rotate." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_13

HEUR-BS-129: "Use email FIRST for JV outreach. If that doesn't work, escalate to offline methods: telegram, send-a-ball, fake newspaper clipping." | domain: networking | source: Issue_03

HEUR-BS-130: "Test smaller, cheaper solo ad lists first ($75 for 16,000 people) before scaling to expensive ones." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_07

HEUR-BS-131: "For a list that hasn't been mailed in weeks/months, send 2 warm-up emails before doing any selling." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_05

HEUR-BS-132: "Go back months later and examine your old emails. Think of ways to improve them. Then recycle and send again for even more sales." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-133: "Create your own unique 'lexicon' -- unique words and phrases your market starts associating with you. 'Goo-roo', 'ex-spurt', 'the green stuff'." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue_06

HEUR-BS-134: "Cliffhanger endings increase the chances of your next email being opened. Future-pace them to looking forward to tomorrow's email." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

HEUR-BS-135: "Don't try to use someone else's voice. Match your voice to your market. Weight loss women get different tone than male internet marketers. Salsa dancing gets different tone than martial arts." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_16

---

## L4 FRAMEWORKS (FW-BS-100 to FW-BS-145)

FW-BS-100: "The 4-Step Launch Process: (1) Announce product + drive to sublist, (2) Tease both main list and sublist daily, (3) Launch with tight deadline + eBay countdown, (4) 'Oops bigger discount' close. Simple. No complicated funnels." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

FW-BS-101: "The Personal Email Framework: (1) Tell a genuinely personal story about yourself, (2) Keep it in the prospect's 'world' of pains/desires, (3) Add entertainment value, (4) Include curiosity subject line, (5) End with cliffhanger, (6) Always ask for the sale." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

FW-BS-102: "The Tao of Steve Email Formula: (1) Be Desire-less -- don't telegraph you want the sale, befriend first, (2) Be Excellent -- show value through infotainment and soft teaching, (3) Be Gone -- take it away, make product expensive/exclusive, slap deadlines." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_15

FW-BS-103: "The JV Outreach Email Framework (David Dutton): Short. Warm/friendly. No hidden agenda. Social proof (name-drop). Include phone number. Connect first, ask for favors later." | domain: networking | source: Issue_03

FW-BS-104: "The Media Pitch Email Template: (1) Reference where you saw their listing, (2) Quick segment idea with compelling title in quotes, (3) 3-4 bullet talking points, (4) Brief 'About' bio with credentials, (5) Email + phone." | domain: publicity | source: Issue_03

FW-BS-105: "The Invalidation Email Framework: Pick something your competition does. Show why it's not as good as what you do. Even mock it. Don't name names, just describe. Position yourself as the ONLY rational choice." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

FW-BS-106: "The Credibility Hijack Email Framework: Find testimonial from someone with celebrity appeal. Build subject line around that celebrity. Use testimonial as proof. Name their credentials specifically." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

FW-BS-107: "The Sacred Cow Email Framework: (1) Choose a revered figure/belief in your market, (2) Question or challenge one specific thing they teach, (3) Make your case with logic, (4) Don't insult the person -- just challenge the idea, (5) Pivot to your product/solution." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_09

FW-BS-108: "5 Plug-n-Play Email Templates: (1) The Story Email, (2) The Q&A Email, (3) The Checklist Email, (4) The Controversial Email, (5) The Historical Solution Email. Each with subject line template + body fill-in-the-blanks." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_02

FW-BS-109: "The Blatant Pitch Framework: (1) Start with curiosity-packed subject line, (2) Tease with 'how is this possible?' setup, (3) Reveal just enough mechanism to intrigue, (4) Use take-away close ('don't use if you don't have an open mind'), (5) Link to product." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_12

FW-BS-110: "The Pain Email Framework: (1) Write about the painful problem in vivid detail, (2) No benefits, no claims at all, (3) Describe symptoms and suffering, (4) Simple call to action at end. 'It's the problem, stupid.'" | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_10

FW-BS-111: "The Forum Research Email Framework: (1) Find popular forums in market, (2) Sort by most-viewed posts, (3) Use those titles as subject lines, (4) Use forum language in email body, (5) Adapt stories from forum posts." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_11

FW-BS-112: "The Sequential Selling Framework (Sean D'Souza): Opt-in first > Buy flagship product > Subscribe to continuity > Then (and only then) access other products. Like restaurants: seating > appetizer > main > dessert > coffee." | domain: business-strategy | source: Issue_13

FW-BS-113: "The Micro Commitment Method: Landing page shows survey/dropdown with specific problem choices. Visitor self-segments. Goes to squeeze page + 30-day autoresponder for THAT specific problem. Buyers go to buyer list. Non-buyers after 30 days get deleted." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_13

FW-BS-114: "The Ethically Fibbing Framework: (1) Announce smaller discount (50%), (2) On launch day, reveal actual bigger discount (64%), (3) Frame as genuine 'oops' or change of mind, (4) Keep tone matter-of-fact, not gimmicky, (5) Do this ONCE per list only." | domain: promotions | source: Issue_02

FW-BS-115: "The 'Meet the Master' First Email: Tell your story of woe, show credentials, how you overcame challenges, now you'll share what you know. Gets them to know you, inspires them, sets up future emails." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_04

FW-BS-116: "The Humor Email Architecture: Use cruel irony, sarcasm, mocking competition, sexual innuendo, high contrast, unusual word choices, play on words, foreign slang -- then connect to product." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_06

FW-BS-117: "The Time Continuum Landing Page: Use present-tense '-ing' verbs. Words like 'right now', 'at this very moment'. Replace 'will' and 'can' with present tense. Create BELIEF + IMMEDIATE GRATIFICATION + CURRENT EVENT + VIVID DESCRIPTION." | domain: conversion-optimization | source: Issue_08

FW-BS-118: "The Loser vs Winner Email Template: List 8-10 contrasting pairs of 'The loser says X / The winner says Y' using repetitive structure. Reader self-identifies with winner. Hypnotic rhythm." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_16

FW-BS-119: "The 90-Day Email Mastery Program (adapted from PUA): (1) Start daily writing workout, (2) Get one great resource, (3) Write 90 emails fast, (4) Watch Seinfeld daily, (5) Keep a notebook of what works, (6) Re-read your best resources 20x, (7) After 90 emails, reflect 2 weeks, (8) Repeat." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_14

---

## L5 METHODOLOGIES (MET-BS-100 to MET-BS-130)

MET-BS-100: "Complete Email Product Launch Methodology: Week 1-2: Warm-up emails (if list is cold). Week 3-6: Daily tease emails driving to sublist + main list. Pre-launch day: invalidation email. Launch day: announce + bigger discount reveal. Launch days 2-3: reminder emails. Final day: eBay countdown with multiple emails same day (morning, afternoon, evening, midnight). All emails under 300 words. Fantasy-forward. In prospect's 'world'." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

MET-BS-101: "The JV List Building Methodology: (1) Find 5-10 people (peers with similar-sized lists, not just gurus), (2) Send David Dutton-style email with social proof + phone number, (3) Connect with no agenda, befriend first, (4) If email fails, escalate to telegram/send-a-ball/fake newspaper, (5) One endorsed mailing = 100+ subscribers. Target: peers with 200-500 person lists selling non-competing products." | domain: list-building | source: Issue_03

MET-BS-102: "The Media Booking Methodology: (1) Sign up at RadioGuestList.com for alerts, (2) When alert matches your topic, send media pitch email within minutes (first responder advantage), (3) Keep master list of bullet talking points and themes saved on computer, (4) For each show, pick matching theme + 3-4 bullets, fill template, send in under 3 minutes, (5) Start local, build credibility, then approach national." | domain: publicity | source: Issue_03

MET-BS-103: "The Forum-to-Email Writing Method: (1) Find 3-5 popular forums where market gathers, (2) Sort posts by most viewed, (3) Study most-viewed titles -- use as subject line templates, (4) Read complaints and stories for exact market language, (5) Adapt stories into emails ('I know of a guy who...' in your own words), (6) Check blog comments too for off-the-cuff hot buttons." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_11

MET-BS-104: "The Daily Email Writing Process: (1) Check forums/news/IMDB for ideas, (2) Pick one topic that intersects with market's world, (3) Write opening using one of 24+ proven openers (joke, FAQ, question, new word, book quote, etc.), (4) Stay in their 'world' of pains/desires, (5) Soft teach only -- tell WHAT not HOW, (6) Plug product naturally in body + PS, (7) Use curiosity subject line, (8) End with cliffhanger if possible. Target: 10-15 minutes per email." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issues_01-16

MET-BS-105: "The 24-Point Email Cheat Sheet (Subject Line Templates): (1) Joke opening, (2) Book piggyback, (3) FAQ open, (4) Industry gossip comment, (5) Whining subscriber profit, (6) 'Why I Don't Do X', (7) 3 Questions opening, (8) New Word opening, (9) 'A Whacky Crazy Way To X', (10) Long subject lines allowed, (11) 'This Email Contains X', (12) 'Great advice I got' opening, (13) 'My Big Stupid X Failure', (14) 'Interesting question I got', (15) 'Things that don't make sense' list, (16) 'I don't know if it's something in the water' PS, (17) Come-get-your-lovin' sale with testimonials, (18) Myths list, (19) '10 Mistakes That Keep Even Smart People X', (20) 'Who Wins In A Fight: X or Y?', (21) 'Most Expensive X', (22) Quiz-style, (23) Shock effect from IMDB forums, (24) Mel Martin fascination templates." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issues_01-11

MET-BS-106: "The Sublist Launch Methodology: (1) Create squeeze page for 'VIP' or 'early bird discount' list, (2) Email main list daily driving to sublist, (3) Monitor demand -- low opt-ins = weak product idea, (4) Sublist gets different emails than main list during launch, (5) On launch day, sublist gets first access at biggest discount, (6) Main list gets regular launch emails, (7) Final day: eBay countdown with multiple emails to BOTH lists." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue_05

MET-BS-107: "The Blatant Pitch Methodology: (1) Crank curiosity to maximum in subject line and opening, (2) Reveal just enough of the mechanism to fascinate ('it's based on a secret way of consuming protein'), (3) Stack 4-5 bullet-point benefits, (4) Use take-away ('not everyone qualifies'), (5) Include proof element (real science, expert credentials), (6) Don't disguise that it's a pitch -- OWN it, (7) Do NOT overuse -- mix with value emails." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_12

MET-BS-108: "The Email Critique Methodology: (1) Check if opening gets in prospect's world immediately, (2) Verify no hard teaching -- only soft teaching (WHAT not HOW), (3) Is there entertainment value?, (4) Is the product plugged in the body (not just PS)?, (5) Is there curiosity driving to the link?, (6) Are sentences short and 'breathing'?, (7) Is there a take-away or future-pace?, (8) Does subject line create curiosity without being hypey?" | domain: email-marketing | source: Issues_02-04

MET-BS-109: "The Sequential Product Line Methodology (from Sean D'Souza): (1) Visitor opts in (1st conversion), (2) Sells ONLY flagship product, (3) After purchase, offers continuity subscription (2nd conversion), (4) After continuity, opens access to full product line (3rd conversion), (5) After 3rd conversion, they buy everything. Model: Star Wars > Empire > Jedi. Never let people skip sequence." | domain: business-strategy | source: Issue_13

MET-BS-110: "The Entertainment Email Writing Method: (1) Find jokes, ironic situations, or funny stories, (2) Look for high-contrast elements ('fat anorexic'), (3) Insert foreign slang or unusual word choices, (4) Build entire email around the entertaining element, (5) Tie back to product at end, (6) OK if email doesn't sell directly -- long-term bonding value is worth it." | domain: email-marketing | source: Issue_06
