## L1 PHILOSOPHIES (FIL-BS-300+)

FIL-BS-300: "The villain defines the hero, not vice versa. Without an evil, powerful, diabolical villain, the hero is just another ordinary chump walking the streets." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 59

FIL-BS-301: "Your product is the hero riding in on his noble steed and slaying the bad guy. But if the bad guy is not seen as a truly credible threat, your product will not look nearly as effective as it really is." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 59

FIL-BS-302: "Ugly has always made me more sales. Ugly, simple, and non-cool looking -- for everything from emails and sales letters, to product packaging and entire websites." | domain: marketing-philosophy | source: Issue 59

FIL-BS-303: "Gene Schwartz specialized in ugly. He pointed out: 'The ugly thing in a world of beauty stands out.' What stands out is more likely to get noticed. And what's noticed is more likely to be bought from." | domain: design-philosophy | source: Issue 59

FIL-BS-304: "All highly successful people are crazy and extreme. If people don't think you're nuts you're simply not trying hard enough. Push the limits." (via Wall Street Playboys) | domain: email-mindset | source: Issue 60

FIL-BS-305: "There's a big difference between email writers who play to not lose and who play to win. Those who play to not lose hold back, live in fear, and never make the sales they could." | domain: email-mindset | source: Issue 60

FIL-BS-306: "The ONLY purpose of a website (direct response marketing website) should be to build a list -- anything that detracts from that should be left out." | domain: list-building | source: Issue 61

FIL-BS-307: "All roads lead to building the list on a direct response marketing website. All those feel-good reasons -- building trust, educating, building a brand -- ultimately serve to put visitors on your list or buy something." | domain: list-building | source: Issue 61

FIL-BS-308: "If they are in heaven and comfy, you take their halo, pluck out their wings, and cast them down into the abyss! Nobody needs a hero if there's no villain." | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue 61

FIL-BS-309: "Personal branding isn't just a logo or identity -- it is everything you do to attract and maintain customer relationships -- including customer service, how your assistant answers the phone, your products, your name and reputation." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue 62

FIL-BS-310: "Far and away the most important personal brand building thing you can do is specialization. Specialization sets you apart, lets you charge more, gives you built-in credibility, and makes people think you are better than anyone else." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue 62

FIL-BS-311: "Build your business to suit your preferences. If you don't want customers who are serial refunders, build your brand in such a way where those types want nothing to do with you." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue 62

FIL-BS-312: "It IS the prospect's fault. In most cases it is fundamentally dishonest to say 'it's not your fault.' Copy has to be rooted in honesty." | domain: copywriting-ethics | source: Issue 62

FIL-BS-313: "Entertain or die. Status is directly related to how happy you make others feel. Entertainers are always higher status than anyone else." (via A.B. Dada) | domain: email-philosophy | source: Issue 65

FIL-BS-314: "I always strive to entertain first, teach second. Trying to position yourself as a great teacher before being seen as a great entertainer is a huge mistake." | domain: content-philosophy | source: Issue 65

FIL-BS-315: "Principles override tactics. Tactics without principles are a joke to anyone who has both. Most marketing is all tactics. Jim Camp was all about principles first." | domain: marketing-philosophy | source: Issue 66

FIL-BS-316: "Don't obsess over specific outcomes. Be outcome independent. Focus on what you can control -- doing the right things long enough consistently -- and you'll get to where you're going with a lot less anxiety." (via Jim Camp) | domain: business-mindset | source: Issue 66

FIL-BS-317: "You're always safe in the other person's world. As long as you are in THEIR world, dealing with THEIR vision and problems and desires, you cannot really bore them." (via Jim Camp) | domain: selling-philosophy | source: Issue 66

FIL-BS-318: "The best way to get a yes is to get them telling you no. Trying to get a no is, paradoxically, the best way to get a yes." (via Jim Camp) | domain: negotiation | source: Issue 66

FIL-BS-319: "Insecurity is good motivation." (via Steve McQueen) "Insecurities motivate all of us to take action -- to either conquer them or hide them. If you use this quirk of human nature in your emails it can do wonders for your sales." | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue 66

FIL-BS-320: "The math is always on your side. There is simply less supply of you than there are leads, clients, customers that will buy. If you are a talented freelancer, you are in the catbird seat." | domain: business-mindset | source: Issue 67

FIL-BS-321: "The time to build your arc is when the sun's out and everything looks great. When it starts raining, it's too late." (Doberman Dan) | domain: business-resilience | source: Ravings of Adman Pt2

FIL-BS-322: "Client work should be a choice, not a need. Your mission (your own product line) is paramount. Clients are a compliment to your life, something you allow in your world." | domain: business-philosophy | source: Issue 67

## L2 MENTAL MODELS (MM-BS-300+)

MM-BS-300: "Disney Villain Model -- Every negative emotion and every pain your prospect experiences is a villain. The villain determines how much you like the hero. Fill your 'Rogues Gallery' of villains attacking your market." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 59

MM-BS-301: "Double Tap Email Strategy -- Morning email = inspiration (good feelings, motivation). Evening email = insecurity (wrench on pain with reckless abandon). This combo took a client from $0 to $100K+ in December." | domain: email-strategy | source: Issue 60

MM-BS-302: "Drudge Report Model -- The ugliest site on the Internet is also the #1 leading source of referral traffic. Only two skills: clarity and headlines. Think on that next time you're going to spend a fortune on something fancy." | domain: marketing-design | source: Issue 59

MM-BS-303: "Steve McQueen Scene-Stealing Model -- McQueen would 'say' pages of dialogue with a single look. He'd toss out 2-3 pages of dialogue. In emails, using idioms you can 'say' in a sentence or two far more than 3 paragraphs of drivel." | domain: email-writing | source: Issue 60

MM-BS-304: "Heaven-to-Hell Casting Model -- When prospects haven't bought yet, cast them from comfort down into the abyss in your emails. 'Make them see the hearse pulling up to the curb.'" | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue 61

MM-BS-305: "5th Stage Email Model -- Based on Gene Schwartz's Breakthrough Advertising 5th stage of market awareness. Emails that don't look like selling, don't promise anything, just 'echo an emotion.' Most profitable for long-term daily emailing." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 65

MM-BS-306: "David Lynch 'Severed Ear' Model -- Pepper your emails with unusual, random, weird stuff that sucks attention in. Always have something off-key grabbing attention. Disturb, offend, or mystify." | domain: email-attention | source: Issue 62

MM-BS-307: "Peter Montoya Personal Brand Strength Litmus Test -- (1) How much competition and peers are annoyed by you (2) Does your brand repel as much as it attracts? If it doesn't cause 'I can't stand that guy' reactions, your brand isn't strong enough." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue 62

MM-BS-308: "Peter Montoya's 4 Pillars of Personal Brand Power -- (1) Reach: how many people know you (2) Influence: how many are influenced by you (3) Ability to attract/generate revenue (4) Good will toward man." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue 62

MM-BS-309: "Jim Camp's Vision-Decision Chain -- Vision creates emotion. Decisions are made emotionally. The decision to be 'logical' is itself an emotional decision. Create a vision before pitching." | domain: selling-persuasion | source: Issue 66

MM-BS-310: "Needs More Gore Model -- 10% story and 90% product = wrong. Flip it: massive horror-gore detail about the problem FIRST, then only after the vision is built, the solution." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 66

MM-BS-311: "Merchant Account Diversification Model -- Multiple merchant accounts across different providers (including offshore), rotate charges weekly to keep all active, never put all revenue through one gateway." | domain: business-resilience | source: Ravings Pt1-2

MM-BS-312: "Unusual Facts Email Model -- Pull email ideas from news stories and unusual facts. Dan Kennedy: 'You could build a million dollar per year business simply going through USA Today each day.' Connect the fact to your product." | domain: email-ideas | source: Issue 61/65

---

## L3 HEURISTICS (HEUR-BS-300+)

HEUR-BS-300: "Send two emails per day instead of one. First email in the morning (inspirational), second email in the evening (insecurity-based). Client went from $0 to $100K+ in December with this approach." | domain: email-frequency | source: Issue 60

HEUR-BS-301: "Each application client sells is at $13,000 British pounds. He had 12 applications in one month using the Double Tap method -- well over $220,000 US." | domain: email-results | source: Issue 60

HEUR-BS-302: "Jim Straw sold a $997 eBook and moved an average of 12 per month with ugly plain text emails, no refund guarantee, no social media, regular PayPal." | domain: email-sales | source: Issue 59

HEUR-BS-303: "Drudge Report gets over 2 million visitors per day. 87% of visitors stay for 30+ seconds. All this despite being the ugliest site on the Internet." | domain: ugly-marketing | source: Issue 59

HEUR-BS-304: "We converted 40% of the list to buyers in weight loss. No before-and-after photos, no fancy graphics, nothing pretty. Just ugly and simple." | domain: conversion-rates | source: Issue 59

HEUR-BS-305: "100 podcast leads are worth more than 1,000 leads from practically anywhere else. Podcast listeners tend to be in their 30s and 40s, upper middle class, spend 20-30 minutes listening." | domain: traffic-quality | source: Issue 60

HEUR-BS-306: "Pitch yourself to 3-5 podcast shows per day. Use a simple 3-step method: (1) Simple compliment from show description (2) Look at guests they've had (3) Copy-paste message about how you serve audiences." | domain: podcast-outreach | source: Issue 60

HEUR-BS-307: "Put opt-in forms on every page in multiple locations -- top, bottom, sidebar. Don't allow them to buy anything until they've opted in." | domain: list-building | source: Issue 61

HEUR-BS-308: "Create an opt-in bribe with a real high retail value and perceived value -- offer something people have paid for, and the more they paid the better your results." | domain: list-building | source: Issue 61

HEUR-BS-309: "Position yourself as a leader by giving yourself a title. It took me 14+ years to finally do it, and it made a huge difference." | domain: positioning | source: Issue 60

HEUR-BS-310: "Rush Limbaugh says 50% of his audience can't stand him. Same with Howard Stern. If your brand doesn't cause that reaction in certain people, your brand isn't strong enough." | domain: polarization | source: Issue 62

HEUR-BS-311: "American Giant has an $89 sweatshirt with a six month wait. They BRAG about the wait time because of popularity (social proof). Making the skeleton dance." | domain: selling-flaws | source: Issue 65

HEUR-BS-312: "Barry Maher skeleton dance for fees: 'Are my fees expensive? Absolutely. Why do I charge so much? Because I can -- because my clients are happy to pay them for the kind of results I generate. I'm thinking of raising them.'" | domain: pricing | source: Issue 65

HEUR-BS-313: "Avis ad: 'We're number 2. Therefore we try harder.' Pure genius -- bragging about being #2." | domain: selling-flaws | source: Issue 65

HEUR-BS-314: "Most people never go too far with their emails -- they're too scared or too professional. You won't know what's 'too far' until you've gone too far at least once." | domain: email-boldness | source: Issue 62

HEUR-BS-315: "Don't measure daily sales. Measure monthly and yearly trends. Focus on what you CAN control: writing better emails, creating better offers, bonding more with your list." | domain: metrics | source: Issue 66

HEUR-BS-316: "Jim Camp selling water filters: Failed for 3 weeks with Zig Ziglar-type tactics. Then tossed the script, asked 'How does your water treat your hair?' -- created vision. Made more money part-time than as an airline pilot." | domain: selling-vision | source: Issue 66

HEUR-BS-317: "Get detailed about the problem, be vague about the solution. The essence of great emails: talk a lot about problems, give very little detail about the solution." | domain: email-structure | source: Issue 62

HEUR-BS-318: "When you tell people to get off your list, do it via Q&A email answering someone's complaint. Everyone eavesdrops on the conversation. Doesn't sound like grandstanding." | domain: list-management | source: Issue 59

HEUR-BS-319: "When product is out of stock: (1) Put up squeeze page saying out of stock (2) Offer notification + discount for opt-in (3) Write emails as if selling but direct to squeeze page (4) When back in stock, mail that sublist 2-3x per day with firm deadline." | domain: scarcity-tactics | source: Issue 59

HEUR-BS-320: "Have multiple merchant accounts (3-4 including one offshore). Rotate charges weekly. Keep all active. A single shutdown can kill your business overnight." | domain: business-resilience | source: Ravings Pt1

HEUR-BS-321: "When merchant account volume grows fast, call their security department regularly. Get to know everyone by name, their families, their hobbies. This personal relationship kept one account alive through 2,400% growth." | domain: business-relationships | source: Ravings Pt1

HEUR-BS-322: "Withhold interest until their interest for you is higher than yours is for them. 'The minute you show interest, she wonders if she can do better.' Same applies to clients." | domain: negotiation | source: Issue 67

HEUR-BS-323: "Don't revolve around a client like a planet circling the sun. That's the fastest way to end up in the friend zone. Same in business -- if you revolve around a client, they'll automatically wonder if they can do better." | domain: client-management | source: Issue 67

## L4 FRAMEWORKS (FW-BS-300+)

FW-BS-300: "Rogues Gallery Framework -- Create a comprehensive list of all villains (pains) attacking your market. Categories: Physical Pain, Emotional Pain, Loneliness, Anger/Betrayal/Outrage, Fear, Frustration. Write about a different villain each day." | domain: email-content | source: Issue 59

FW-BS-301: "Double Tap Email Framework -- (1) Morning email: Inspirational (generates good feelings, motivation, excitement) (2) Evening email: Insecurity-based (picks an insecurity, wrenches on it with reckless abandon). Both with strong call to action." | domain: email-strategy | source: Issue 60

FW-BS-302: "Personal Brand Evaluation Framework -- (1) Ask friends/family/clients what image pops in their head when they hear your name (2) What words and attributes? (3) What positive AND negative things? (4) Strong brand = specific feedback; weak brand = vague answers." | domain: personal-branding | source: Issue 62

FW-BS-303: "5th Stage Email Framework -- Based on Schwartz's 5 stages of market awareness. For the most sophisticated/aware market: (1) Don't mention product in subject line (2) Start with emotion/fear/desire/insecurity (3) Build up story (4) Only then mention solution. The email with highest clicks and sales used this." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 65

FW-BS-304: "Making the Skeleton Dance Framework (Barry Maher) -- (1) Identify product's flaws/negatives (2) Instead of hiding them, BRAG about them (3) Turn each negative into a reason to buy. Examples: long wait = high demand, expensive = premium results, #2 = try harder." | domain: selling | source: Issue 65

FW-BS-305: "Unusual Facts to Email Framework (Gary Halbert inspired) -- (1) Find an unusual/interesting fact (2) Use it as the email opener (3) Connect it to your product/market (4) Pivot to your pitch. Works for any niche." | domain: email-content | source: Issue 65

FW-BS-306: "News Story Email Framework -- (1) Find a relevant news story (2) Mention the article (3) Give your 'take' on it (4) Plug your product. 'Among the most profitable kinds of emails.' Can be used in any niche." | domain: email-content | source: Issue 61

FW-BS-307: "Holiday History Email Framework (Doberman Dan example) -- (1) Tell dramatic historical story behind the holiday (2) Connect to current problems (3) Pivot to your solution. Can be reused every year unchanged." | domain: email-content | source: Issue 62

FW-BS-308: "Testimonial Email Template -- Subject line: (whatever - use curiosity when in doubt). [Name] uses [Product] to get [Benefit]: [Testimonial]. Grab [Product] by [Deadline] for [Discount]: [LINK]. Keep it above the fold." | domain: email-templates | source: Issue 60

FW-BS-309: "Podcast Lead Generation Framework -- (1) Go into iTunes, find new shows (2) Write 3-step pitch: compliment + look at guests + copy-paste message about serving audiences (3) Do this for 3-5 shows/day (4) Not the size of the show but the quality of listenership." | domain: traffic | source: Issue 60

FW-BS-310: "Jim Camp Principles-First Marketing Framework -- (1) Know your market deeply first (2) Let email strategy and copy flow from market knowledge (3) Create vision of the problem before pitching (4) Be outcome independent (5) Get them to tell you 'no' to draw out truth." | domain: selling-philosophy | source: Issue 66

FW-BS-311: "Merchant Account Diversification Framework -- (1) Primary merchant account (2) Backup #1 (different provider) (3) Backup #2 (different gateway) (4) Offshore account (Royal Bank of Scotland/Worldpay type) (5) Rotate weekly to keep all active (6) Budget ~$50/month per dormant account as insurance." | domain: business-resilience | source: Ravings Pt1-2

FW-BS-312: "Income Diversification Framework -- (1) Main product line (bulk of income) (2) Joint venture business (separate merchant/PayPal) (3) Kindle books (paid via Amazon/publisher check) (4) Client work (separate income stream, unconnected to your merchant accounts). Goal: each stream making roughly equal." | domain: business-resilience | source: Ravings Pt2

---

## L5 METHODOLOGIES (MET-BS-300+)

MET-BS-300: "Out-of-Stock Product Launch Methodology -- Step 1: Replace sales page with squeeze page saying 'out of stock' + opt-in for notification + discount. Step 2: Write daily emails as if selling but send to squeeze page. Step 3: When restocked, mail sublist 2-3 emails/day for a weekend with firm discount deadline." | domain: product-launch | source: Issue 59

MET-BS-301: "Double Tap Daily Email System -- Step 1: Identify if your market is emotional. Step 2: Send first email in morning at usual time (inspirational, get them emotionally invested). Step 3: Send second email ~12 hours later in evening (insecurity email, wrench on specific insecurity). Step 4: Both emails have clear call to action. Step 5: Rinse and repeat daily. Step 6: For non-emotional markets, test different combos from Email Players Playbook (e.g., random morning + testimonial evening)." | domain: email-system | source: Issue 60

MET-BS-302: "Mouth Breather Subject Line System -- Step 1: Write a subject line so creepy readers wonder if it's a personal email. Step 2: Make them wonder if you're watching them somehow. Step 3: Create split-second 'holy shnikes, is he/she watching me?' reaction. Step 4: Pay it off fast in body copy (connect to content). Step 5: Modulate to your market. Examples: 'Stop watching porn', 'I think about you in the shower', 'Talk dirty to me, [first name]', 'Why do you keep watching my videos?'" | domain: subject-lines | source: Issue 60

MET-BS-303: "Website-as-List-Builder Optimization System -- Step 1: Put opt-in forms on every page (top, bottom, sidebar). Step 2: Don't allow buying until opted in. Step 3: If product links on site, shunt visitors to opt-in page first. Step 4: Keep media content on your server (not YouTube). Step 5: Every podcast/interview plugs main site, not product page. Step 6: Create high-value opt-in bribe (something people have paid for). Step 7: Position yourself with a title. Step 8: Test different photos constantly." | domain: list-building | source: Issue 61

MET-BS-304: "5th Stage Email Methodology -- Step 1: Study Gene Schwartz's market awareness stages. Step 2: Identify the dominant insecurity/emotion/fear in your market. Step 3: Write subject line that has NOTHING to do with your product. Step 4: Open with a story about that emotion (not the product category). Step 5: Build up the story, creating vision and emotion. Step 6: Only when emotion peaks, pivot to your product as the solution. Step 7: Make the pivot feel natural, not forced. Example: MLM product sold via story about stay-at-home dad losing wife -- highest clicks and sales of entire campaign." | domain: email-copywriting | source: Issue 65

MET-BS-305: "Making the Skeleton Dance in Emails -- Step 1: List all negatives/flaws of your product (price, wait time, difficulty, etc). Step 2: For each negative, find the positive reason behind it. Step 3: Bragging format: 'Are we [negative]? Absolutely. Here's why...' Step 4: Use in emails, sales letters, and any marketing. Step 5: Can combine with 'no refund' policy, high prices, etc. Step 6: Tell them about your commission (affiliate): 'I don't want you to forget I'm on commission. The more you spend the more I make. Now let me tell you why you need to be spending more.'" | domain: selling | source: Issue 65

MET-BS-306: "Horror Gore Email Methodology -- Step 1: Find or receive a horror story from your market. Step 2: Get extremely detailed about the pain, consequences, and gore. Step 3: Build vision: what if X had happened? What would life be like? Step 4: Make it 90% gore/story, 10% product mention. Step 5: Before every email, ask: 'Is there enough hardcore gore in this email?' Step 6: Proceed accordingly." | domain: email-persuasion | source: Issue 66

MET-BS-307: "Jim Camp 'Get Them to Say No' Email Methodology -- Step 1: In emails, go out of your way to say product is expensive. Step 2: State you have no refund guarantee. Step 3: Say it's not intended for everyone. Step 4: Do this out of principle (not tactically). Step 5: This eliminates neediness from your voice/words. Step 6: People who DO buy appreciate the product better, don't care about price, care more about quality. Step 7: Paradoxically, this approach increases sales long-term." | domain: email-selling | source: Issue 66

MET-BS-308: "Merchant Account Emergency Preparedness System -- Step 1: Get 3+ merchant accounts (different providers, different gateways). Step 2: Get at least 1 offshore (e.g., Worldpay/Royal Bank of Scotland, ~2.5% rates). Step 3: Rotate charges weekly across all accounts. Step 4: Budget ~$50/month per backup for peace of mind. Step 5: Call security department proactively when volume spikes. Step 6: Never close an account without verifying all pending charges are settled. Step 7: Diversify income streams across unconnected payment methods." | domain: business-operations | source: Ravings Pt1-2

## EVOLUTION OF BEN SETTLE'S THINKING (2011-2017)

**Early Period (2011-2012, Ravings of an Adman):**
- Focus on tactical business operations: merchant accounts, payment processing, list building logistics
- Joint discussions with Doberman Dan about survival/resilience
- More raw, practical, less theoretical
- Heavy emphasis on business diversification and paranoia about single points of failure

**Middle Period (2015-2016, Issues 59-62):**
- Crystallization of the "Ugly Email" philosophy with multiple proof points
- Development of the Disney Villain Model and Rogues Gallery concept
- Extensive practical examples across niches (weight loss, prostate, golf, dating, self-defense)
- Introduction of Double Tap (2x daily email) system
- Strong emphasis on personal branding via Peter Montoya's teachings
- "Get off my list" philosophy refined (Q&A format vs. grandstanding)

**Advanced Period (2016-2017, Issues 65-67):**
- Integration of Jim Camp's negotiation philosophy into email marketing
- Shift from tactics to principles-based thinking
- 5th Stage Email concept (Gene Schwartz integration)
- "Making the Skeleton Dance" formalized
- Deep exploration of insecurity-based selling as a principle (not tactic)
- "Get them to say No" applied to emails and business
- Outcome independence philosophy
- Vision-before-pitching as core principle

## L1 PHILOSOPHIES (Core beliefs)

FIL-BS-001: "The more prolific the writing, the more frequent the hiring" â€” prolific output is the cause; clients are the effect | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FIL-BS-002: "Knowing copywriting and getting clients are two totally different separate skills" â€” writing ability and business development are distinct competencies | domain: freelance-business | source: ECM

FIL-BS-003: "You don't have to be the best copywriter to get hired" â€” positioning and demonstration matter more than raw skill level | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FIL-BS-004: "The freelance copywriting side is only about 20% of the battle. If they don't have the right offer and list, it doesn't matter how good your copy is" | domain: copywriting | source: ECM

FIL-BS-005: "Being a better marketer is more important than being a better copywriter" â€” marketing mindset trumps writing craft | domain: direct-response | source: ECM

FIL-BS-006: "Email is really talk radio on glass" â€” email should be treated like a talk radio show segment, not formal writing | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM, EPC

FIL-BS-007: "People are not craving content anymore. Content is a dime a dozen. What they're not getting is a good experience" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

FIL-BS-008: "It is your moral and ethical obligation to sell. If you have something that somebody needs, it's your job to sell it to them" | domain: selling | source: EPM, ECM

FIL-BS-009: "Never, ever, ever hard teach in your emails" â€” hard teaching creates fans but not buyers, attracts hostile freebie seekers | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-010: "People follow leaders and people buy from leaders. People are going to buy from the leader before they buy from the expert" | domain: positioning | source: EPM

FIL-BS-011: "Neediness is the most insidious thing you can have. It will kill any persuasion power dead" | domain: selling | source: ECM

FIL-BS-012: "Play to win, not play to not lose" â€” worrying about unsubscribes is playing not to lose | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-013: "Curiosity is even more important in advertising than self-interest" â€” people will buy from an ad because of a bullet point on page 18 they can't sleep without knowing | domain: copywriting | source: EPM

FIL-BS-014: "The only wrong thing you can do with email is not send one" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-015: "People want what they can't have. The harder you are to get to, the more value they're going to put on you" | domain: positioning | source: ECM

FIL-BS-016: "Copywriting did not start with Facebook and the internet. It was going on 100 years ago" â€” understand industry history or be doomed to fail | domain: copywriting | source: ECM

FIL-BS-017: "The secret of persuasion is simply finding out what people want and then showing them how to get it" (via Bernard Baruch) | domain: persuasion | source: EPC

FIL-BS-018: "If they don't have some skin in the game when they learn something from you, they're probably not going to apply it" â€” free information doesn't get implemented | domain: selling | source: EPM

FIL-BS-019: "Everything in real life is an email. Anything bad that happens to you is always an email story" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

FIL-BS-020: "Email has nothing to do with being a good writer. It has everything to do with being a good communicator" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-021: "Teachers make almost no money in this society. People who sell make the money" | domain: selling | source: EPC

FIL-BS-022: "You're always safe in the other person's world" (via Jim Camp) â€” talk about their crabgrass, not your weed killer | domain: persuasion | source: EPM, EPC

FIL-BS-023: "Worrying about unsubscribes is like playing to not lose instead of playing to win" â€” want subscribers hot or cold, not lukewarm | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-024: "The very first email you send out should have something for sale. The whole idea of incubating and moving free lines is absolutely retarded" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

FIL-BS-025: "Be an original you, not a warmed-over version of me or anyone else" â€” never swipe emails; keep them personality-driven | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-026: "One dramatic demonstration will get rid of all skepticism, all objections, all questions and get people to buy" (via Gary Bencivenga) | domain: selling | source: ECM

FIL-BS-027: "Don't tell me about your weed killer. Tell me about my crabgrass" â€” focus on the prospect's problems, not your solution features | domain: copywriting | source: ECM, EPC

FIL-BS-028: "I don't strive to be a perfectionist. I strive to be a greatest" (via James Cameron) â€” great > perfect for email | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-029: "Money is attracted to speed" â€” write fast, ship fast, don't spend 2 hours on an email | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FIL-BS-030: "Vision drives decision" (via Jim Camp) â€” you're not giving facts or a PowerPoint, you're painting vision of their problems and desired outcomes | domain: persuasion | source: EPC

FIL-BS-031: "If you can't hide the family skeleton, you might as well make it dance" (via George Bernard Shaw, taught by Barry Maher) â€” brag about your flaws and high prices | domain: pricing-psychology | source: ECM

FIL-BS-032: "You should be very specific about the problem in your ad copy and very vague about the solution" (via Parris Lampropoulos) | domain: copywriting | source: ECM

FIL-BS-033: "Contrast is big for getting attention. The human brain neurologically causes some kind of chaos when two contrasting things are presented" | domain: copywriting | source: EPM

FIL-BS-034: "People like to be teased. If you think people don't like to be teased, we are in a city where guys pay money to be teased" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM, ECM

## L2 MENTAL MODELS (Thinking frameworks)

MM-BS-001: **Law of Cause and Effect** â€” Earl Nightingale principle: lots of writing (cause) = clients finding you (effect). Applies to all prolific output | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

MM-BS-002: **BookMarshall Model** (via Victor Cheng) â€” a book that is simultaneously a commercial for your services. Content that demonstrates knowledge while selling | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

MM-BS-003: **Funded Proposal Model** (via Mike Dillard/Magnetic Sponsoring) â€” create a product that sells generic value, builds your list, and pre-qualifies people to hire you. They buy the product, consume it, then want to hire the producer | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

MM-BS-004: **Cereal Box Positioning** â€” eye-level cereals are not the best tasting, healthiest, or cheapest but outsell everything because of positioning. Same with copywriters | domain: positioning | source: ECM

MM-BS-005: **A-Pile vs B-Pile** (via Gary Halbert) â€” your email should look like personal mail (A-pile), not obvious commercial mail (B-pile) | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MM-BS-006: **Talk Radio Model** â€” email = daily talk radio show. Come in for a 2-3 minute segment, give opinion on something, put a little commercial at the end (your link) | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MM-BS-007: **Hot Dog That Nourishes Like Broccoli** (via Don Hewitt/60 Minutes) â€” make content look fun and tasty (like a hot dog) but still give nourishment (like broccoli) | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MM-BS-008: **Repulsion Marketing** â€” instead of attracting everyone, actively repel people you don't want. Take the sale away. Weed out cheapskates and freebie seekers | domain: positioning | source: ECM

MM-BS-009: **Making the Skeleton Dance** (via Barry Maher/George Bernard Shaw) â€” not just admit flaws, but explain why the flaw is actually good for the customer | domain: pricing-psychology | source: ECM

MM-BS-010: **Client Imprisonment Page** â€” a squeeze page that traps prospects into your email world. Like Shawshank Redemption â€” they become happy prisoners who don't want to leave | domain: list-building | source: ECM

MM-BS-011: **Interweaving Model** (via Gene Schwartz's Breakthrough Advertising) â€” seamlessly merge promotion with content so people can't tell the difference | domain: email-marketing | source: ECM

MM-BS-012: **Green Hulk Secret** â€” Marvel changed the Hulk from green to gray, readers complained but kept buying. Controversy generates sustained engagement from both supporters and detractors | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MM-BS-013: **Racking a Shotgun** â€” a good subject line is like the sound of a shotgun being racked behind someone. Nobody can ignore it. It freezes people | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MM-BS-014: **Top Gun Recruitment Effect** â€” the movie never said "join the Navy" but Navy recruitment went through the roof. Stories sell without hard pitching | domain: storytelling | source: EPM

MM-BS-015: **Email Traffic Snowball** â€” one daily email becomes: an article, a video, a podcast episode, a forum post. One piece of content multiplied across channels | domain: list-building | source: ECM

MM-BS-016: **Jumping in Puddles vs Ocean** (via Dan Kennedy) â€” better to jump up and down in puddles you own than trying to own the whole ocean. Small, owned audiences beat massive uncontrolled ones | domain: list-building | source: ECM

MM-BS-017: **Damaging Admission** (via David Ogilvy) â€” admitting flaws builds trust. Ogilvy made it a cornerstone of his agency after a furniture salesman honestly pointed out a scratch | domain: selling | source: ECM

MM-BS-018: **Cross-Dressing Copywriter** â€” when writing for markets you don't personally belong to (e.g., overweight women), go into forums and read their problems in their language | domain: copywriting | source: EPM

MM-BS-019: **Jazz Musician Model** â€” email isn't a static sales letter. You must keep coming up with new combinations like a jazz musician. Same approach gets stale fast | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

## L3 HEURISTICS (Practical rules, thresholds)

HEUR-BS-001: "Write one email per day, every day. Not even Sundays off. Be better than the postman" | domain: email-marketing | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-002: "Ideal email length is about 250-300 words, always under 400. Like a woman's skirt: long enough to cover the details, short enough to get attention" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM, EPC

HEUR-BS-003: "The from line is the most important part of an email, more important than the subject line. Put your real name, not a business name" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-004: "Test to the sale, not to the open rate. Opens don't put food on the table. Sales do" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-005: "Write emails that can be written in as little as four minutes using the forum secret" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-006: "Every email must have a clear call to action. Do NOT hide it in the PS" | domain: email-marketing | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-007: "Two emails a day is better than one â€” one in the morning, one at night â€” if you have the time" | domain: email-marketing | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-008: "101 emails in an autoresponder sequence, one per day. Write 6 per day to finish in a month" | domain: email-marketing | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-009: "Plain text emails outperform HTML. They look like personal emails, not commercial ones" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

HEUR-BS-010: "Always book yourself in advance and never take rush jobs" (via Richard Armstrong) â€” rush jobs signal neediness | domain: freelance-business | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-011: "Publish your schedule. The more booked you are, the more people want to hire you" | domain: positioning | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-012: "Bait the hook 90% â€” give 90% of the tip away but leave 10% off. They need the product to execute" (via David Doge) | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-013: "Always plug something in every email. A link to something you're selling. The 'goodwill email' with no pitch is a mistake" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM, EPC

HEUR-BS-014: "Avoid the word 'now' in closing CTAs. Use 'today' or a specific time instead. Tested and proven by Jim Yaghi" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

HEUR-BS-015: "Contrast the look of your paragraphs: one block of 4-5 lines, then one word, then one line. Easy on the eyes" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-016: "Prep your list before switching to daily: tell them you're mailing more often. Let the ones who don't want it leave" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-017: "Good products to study: listen to anything 10+ times. You'll trace tens of thousands of dollars to one good product consumed deeply" | domain: learning | source: EPC, ECM

HEUR-BS-018: "Emails need more gore. Stories need to be dramatic, painful, horror-story-level. Bury the lead = lose the sale" | domain: storytelling | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-019: "Your book/BookMarshall should exist in both print AND ebook. Establish a retail price on ClickBank or Amazon to give it legitimate value" | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-020: "All roads lead to list. Only promote your squeeze page. Never send your list to someone else's cash register (YouTube, Facebook)" | domain: list-building | source: ECM, EPM

HEUR-BS-021: "When someone asks 'Why should I hire you?' â€” FedEx them your book. Let the book do the selling over the weekend" (via Bob Bly) | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-022: "Specialize if you can. Health market, financial market, make-money market. Specialization attracts higher quality prospects" | domain: freelance-business | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-023: "Tell people what to do but not how to do it. That's the art of soft teaching" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

HEUR-BS-024: "Don't worry about typos. If somebody complains about a typo, that's their problem, not yours" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-025: "Never give price without establishing value first. That's copywriting 101" | domain: pricing-psychology | source: ECM

HEUR-BS-026: "Use figures of speech, slang, Australian slang, Old English â€” pepper personality into emails. Get a little goofy" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-027: "Unsubscribes save you money. Auto-responder companies charge more as your list grows. Get rid of lukewarm people" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-028: "30-Day Double Your Sales Plan: write one email per day for 30 days (or 5/week). You will see an uptake in sales" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

HEUR-BS-029: "If you're going to use a shock subject line, pay it off fast in the first sentence" | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

## L4 FRAMEWORKS (Structured approaches)

FW-BS-001: **10 Subject Line Types** â€” (1) Shock Effect, (2) Curiosity, (3) Straight Benefit, (4) Bizarre, (5) Plain Terms/Phrases/Quotes, (6) Contrast, (7) Play on Books/Songs/Movies, (8) TV/Movie Characters, (9) Challenging Dogma, (10) Mix and Match | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-002: **7 Newbie-Proof Email Types** â€” (1) Infotainment emails, (2) Bait the Hook 90%, (3) Tease emails, (4) Problem-focused emails, (5) Forum Secret (4-minute emails), (6) Inspiration emails, (7) Q&A emails; plus Checklists, Controversy emails, Stories/Metaphors/Analogies | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-003: **Email Client Machine System** â€” 9 ways to rig it so prospects are 80%+ sold before you talk to them: (1) Build client imprisonment page, (2) Become the new mailman (daily email), (3) Create your BookMarshall, (4) Use call to action matching your strength, (5) Write with gore/drama, (6) Interweave content and promotion, (7) Use repulsion marketing, (8) Make the skeleton dance on pricing, (9) Establish waiting lists | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FW-BS-004: **5 Benefits of BookMarshall** â€” (1) Positions you as a for-real expert, (2) Showcases your writing, (3) Demonstrates your knowledge, (4) Builds credibility by default, (5) Sells your services | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FW-BS-005: **Jim Camp Price Deflection** â€” when asked about price: "I'm expensive. Let's talk about your business first. If at any time you want to tell me no, that's perfectly fine. Will you do me a favor and tell me no whenever you want?" | domain: pricing-psychology | source: ECM

FW-BS-006: **Four-Minute Email Forum Secret** â€” (1) Go to forums in your niche, (2) Sort threads by views, (3) Find highest-viewed threads, (4) Use the thread title as subject line, (5) Rewrite the problem/story from the thread in your own words | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-007: **Email Traffic Snowball** â€” take one daily email and: (1) Add soft teaching to make it an article, (2) Post on article directories, (3) Record as video for YouTube, (4) Extract audio for podcast on iTunes, (5) Post on niche forums, (6) Share on social media. All roads lead back to squeeze page | domain: list-building | source: ECM

FW-BS-008: **The Case Against [X]** Subject Line (via Gary Bencivenga) â€” take something everyone accepts as fact and say the opposite. "The Case Against Saw Palmetto." Forces readers to open because their identity is wrapped up in the belief | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-009: **Client Acquisition Through Writing** â€” (1) Write prolifically, (2) Compile writing into a BookMarshall, (3) Put BookMarshall for sale AND free on squeeze page, (4) Build email list, (5) Mail daily with infotainment, (6) Use repulsion to filter, (7) Clients come to you pre-sold | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FW-BS-010: **Waiting List Strategy** â€” when booked up: (1) Replace CTA with "get on my waiting list," (2) Creates feeding frenzy of 2-4 top clients, (3) Pick your favorite, (4) Gives even stronger positioning, (5) Later, send overflow to apprentice copywriters for a percentage | domain: positioning | source: ECM

FW-BS-011: **Soft Teaching Formula** â€” tell them WHAT to do but not HOW. Example: "People who drink more water lose weight. But you need to know how much water to drink, because too much has the opposite effect. You want to make sure you drink the right kind..." Then link to product | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

FW-BS-012: **3 Ways to Create Your BookMarshall Fast** â€” (1) Recycle content you've already written, (2) Pre-write a bunch of daily emails and compile them, (3) Talk into a transcription service (Rev.com) and assemble the transcript | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

FW-BS-013: **Inspiration Email Structure** â€” tell an inspirational story about someone who overcame a problem. Don't hard pitch. Just say "If you'd like to get XYZ benefit, just click this link." People buy from the person who inspired them | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-014: **Checklist Email Types** â€” (1) Tell market what they're doing right, then tease what they're missing, (2) Tell people what they're doing wrong that they think is right (challenge dogma), (3) Quick numbered tips with product tease | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

FW-BS-015: **Interview-to-Email Pipeline** â€” (1) Interview an expert/client for 90 minutes, (2) Get transcript, (3) Pull sections where points are made, (4) Smooth out into story format, (5) Add call to action. Preserves their authentic voice | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

## L5 METHODOLOGIES (Complete systems/processes)

MET-BS-001: **The Email Players Method** â€” Complete daily email monetization system: Foundation: email = talk radio. Never hard teach. Master infotainment. Steps: (1) Build personality-driven brand, (2) Create squeeze page with BookMarshall as opt-in bribe, (3) Mail daily using 10 subject line types, (4) Write using 7 newbie-proof email formats, (5) Always plug something in every email, (6) Use contrast in formatting, (7) Test to the sale not the open, (8) Prep list before going daily, (9) Always be adding new names. Result: position as leader not just expert, create traffic on demand, sell without looking salesy | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

MET-BS-002: **The Email Client Machine** â€” Complete system for freelance copywriters to get clients chasing them: Phase 1 (Foundation): Write prolifically to invoke law of cause and effect. Phase 2 (BookMarshall): Create a book-commercial hybrid. Publish as ebook AND print. Establish retail price. Phase 3 (Client Imprisonment): Build squeeze page offering BookMarshall free for opt-in. Only promote this page everywhere. Phase 4 (Daily Email): Mail every day using infotainment. Include CTA matching your strength (call, consult, webinar). Write with gore/drama. Interweave content and promotion. Phase 5 (Positioning): Use repulsion marketing, make skeleton dance on pricing, publish schedule, create waiting lists. Result: clients 80%+ pre-sold before contact, no pitching/begging/neediness | domain: client-acquisition | source: ECM

MET-BS-003: **Street Smart Email System** â€” The complete email writing system taught via Q&A format: Core principle: make every email fun to read. #1 element = fun. Steps: (1) Keep antenna always up for ideas (everything is a story), (2) Write like you talk, warts and all, (3) Tell stories â€” they're neurologically the easiest way to communicate, (4) Be personal â€” share real experiences, (5) Sell in every email but merge content with promotion, (6) Bait the hook 90%, (7) Use contrast and curiosity in subject lines, (8) Be a jazz musician â€” keep changing combinations, (9) Know your market's language from forums and magazines, (10) Never use swipe files for emails | domain: email-marketing | source: EPC

MET-BS-004: **30-Day Double Your Sales Action Plan** â€” (1) Write one email per day for 30 days (5/week minimum), (2) Use the 7 newbie-proof formats, (3) Prep list first if not used to daily emails, (4) Write fast â€” money is attracted to speed, (5) Strive for great not perfect, (6) Always include link to offer, (7) Track sales not opens | domain: email-marketing | source: EPM

## CHARACTERISTIC VOICE PATTERNS

1. **Conversational/rambling storytelling** â€” long tangential stories that eventually circle back to a point
2. **Constant name-dropping of direct-response legends** â€” Gary Halbert, Dan Kennedy, Gary Bencivenga, John Caples, David Ogilvy, Gene Schwartz, Jim Camp, Parris Lampropoulos, Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly
3. **Contrarian positioning** â€” often states the opposite of conventional wisdom then defends it
4. **Self-deprecating about his own skills** â€” "I was not the best option any of my clients could have hired"
5. **Anti-woo-woo** â€” explicitly rejects "law of attraction" language in favor of "law of physics" / cause and effect
6. **Blunt/direct** â€” "cool your jets," "stop rolling your eyes," "just dumb"
7. **Pop culture analogies** â€” Seinfeld, Spider-Man, Top Gun, Shawshank Redemption, Incredible Hulk, Fonzie
8. **Repetitive emphasis** â€” repeats key phrases multiple times to drive the point home
9. **Anti-guru skepticism** â€” "most gurus don't know what they're doing with email"
10. **Introverted identity** â€” repeatedly states he's introverted, doesn't like networking, prefers writing to talking
